column1: SVAAS — MASTER EXECUTION ENGINE  |  All Tasks, All Phases, Day 1 to Day 730 #
column5: Sub-Task / Exact Action
column6: Notes & Dependencies

column5: Shortlist 3 names through numerology / astrology / catchiness process. Check each: (a) MCA name availability at mca.gov.in (b) Trademark Class 3 at ipindia.gov.in (c) Domain .in availability. Pick name that clears all three.
column6: Name on LLP must match label, trademark, and domain exactly. Do not form LLP before name is confirmed.

column5: Buy primary domain (.in preferred) + backup (.com if available). Register both. Point .com to .in.
column6: Buy immediately once name is decided — domains get squatted. Cost: ₹800–₹1,200/year per domain.

column5: Brief CA on structure: LLP with Founder as primary partner. Agree fees. Provide: PAN, Aadhaar, passport photo, address proof for all partners.
column6: CA will handle MCA filing, LLP agreement draft, and DIN/DPIN. Timeline: 10–14 days after documents submitted.

column4: Get DPIN for all designated partners
column5: CA files DIR-3 KYC or integrated DPIN application on MCA portal. Each partner needs: PAN, Aadhaar, photo, address proof.
column6: Required before LLP can be incorporated.

column5: CA drafts agreement covering: capital contribution, profit sharing, decision rights, IP ownership (all formulas and brand vest in LLP), withdrawal rights, dissolution.
column6: IP clause is critical. Formula and brand must belong to LLP from Day 1, not individual founders.

column5: CA submits FiLLiP on MCA portal. Pay government fee. Receive Certificate of Incorporation (COI) + LLP Identification Number (LLPIN).
column6: COI is needed for bank account, GST, and licence applications. Government fee included in CA charge.

column5: CA applies for PAN for the LLP entity (not founder PAN) immediately after COI.
column6: LLP PAN is separate from founder's personal PAN. Needed for bank account and GST.

column5: Register on GST portal after LLP PAN is received. Cosmetics HSN: 3305 (hair care) / 3307 (bath salts). Rate: 18%. Use CA for filing.
column6: Do not sell commercially without GSTIN. E-invoicing may be required if B2B sales cross threshold.

column5: Register at udyamregistration.gov.in. Free. Links to PAN. Get MSME certificate. Opens access to government schemes and priority lending.
column6: Takes 1 day online. Do it in Week 3 alongside GST.

column5: After COI + PAN: open current account at HDFC / ICICI / Kotak. Request startup zero-balance current account. Bring: COI, LLP agreement, PAN, address proof, partner KYC.
column6: All D2C payments (Razorpay / Cashfree) require current account. Never mix with personal account.

column5: Search IP India (ipindia.gov.in) under Class 3 (cosmetics). Also check Class 5 (Ayurvedic medicines). Search phonetic variants too.
column6: Free search. Do before LLP name is finalised. If conflicting mark found, change the name.

column5: Search IP India Class 3 for Svaaskriti and phonetic variants. Confirm clear before filing.
column6: Sub-brand needs its own trademark protection.

column5: File TM-A form via IP attorney. Small entity fee: ₹4,500. Attorney fee additional. Use (TM) symbol from day of filing.
column6: Protection runs from filing date. Do not wait for grant (takes 18–36 months).

column5: Same process as brand TM. Two separate applications.
column6: File simultaneously to save attorney mobilisation fee.

column4: Log all P0 decisions in Founder Decision Register
column5: For every decision made in Phase 0: record the question, options considered, decision made, rationale, and date. Use the Founder Register sheet in this workbook.
column6: This protects you from second-guessing later. Creates accountability and memory.

column4: Sign formal service agreement between Svaas LLP and Oodles
column5: Define scope: what Oodles provides monthly (design hours, content, photography, website), what is billed separately, turnaround times, who owns created IP (must be Svaas LLP). Even if same owner — sign it.
column6: IP ownership ambiguity is a real risk. All creative work done for Svaas must contractually belong to Svaas LLP.

column4: Create Oodles deliverable tracker for Svaas
column5: Set up a shared tracker (Notion/Google Sheet) listing every Svaas deliverable from Oodles: item, brief date, deadline, status, file location. Review weekly.
column6: Without a tracker, brand work drifts and deadlines are missed. This is the coordination system.

column4: Confirm B.Pharm cousin as Qualified Person
column5: Have a direct conversation. Explain role: named on licence, must be contactable, signs off on batch records periodically. Not required daily. Agree on a nominal engagement fee.
column6: This is the single most important non-financial decision. QP = licence unlocked. Everything waits on this conversation.

column5: One-page letter: role description, batch sign-off responsibility, annual fee, confidentiality, duration. Get signed.
column6: Formalise even with family. Protects both parties.

column4: Assess 150 sq ft room for licence readiness
column5: Walk through with a regulatory consultant or knowledgeable person. Check: (a) Surfaces — washable? (b) Ventilation — adequate for EO handling? (c) Access to water/wash? (d) Separate from general office use? (e) Fire extinguisher position? (f) Light adequacy?
column6: Cosmetic licence inspector will visit. The room must look like a manufacturing space, not a storage room.

column5: Paint walls with washable emulsion. If floor is bare concrete: seal or lay SS-compatible tiles or easy-clean vinyl. Remove all non-manufacturing items.
column6: Inspector needs to see dedicated, cleanable space. Budget for basic preparation.

column5: Mark three zones (tape + printed signs): (1) Raw Material Storage, (2) Manufacturing / Processing, (3) Packaging / Labelling. Even in 150 sq ft — zone markings show process discipline.
column6: Inspectors look for evidence of process separation.

column5: Essential oils are flammable and aromatic. Adequate air exchange is a safety and compliance requirement.
column6: If room already has a window: cross-ventilation may suffice. Otherwise: exhaust fan installation.

column5: 2x ABC dry powder fire extinguisher (2kg each). Place near entry. Ensure staff know location.
column6: Inspector checks for fire safety equipment specifically.

column5: Standard first aid box: bandages, antiseptic, eyewash solution, burn gel. Mount on wall.
column6: Basic safety requirement. Inspector will note absence.

column5: Stainless steel (SS 304 food grade). 20L capacity. Use for oil infusion batches. Source: Chennai SS fabricators (Perambur, Ambattur industrial area) or online (Industry Buying, IndiaMART).
column6: Two vessels allow parallel processing: one for oil infusion, one for herb processing.

column5: Smaller vessels for essential oil blending, bath salt mixing, secondary processing.
column6: Do not use aluminium. Only SS or glass for oil-based products.

column5: Critical for formula accuracy. Capacity: 5kg. Precision: 0.1g. Brand: Phoenix or Essae (Chennai distributors available). Must be calibrated.
column6: Formula is by weight. 'A handful' is not a batch record entry.

column5: For in-process QC. Hair oil target pH: 4.5-6.5. Bath salt water pH: 6.0-7.5. Calibrate with buffer solutions monthly.
column6: CRITICAL — every batch must be pH tested before dispatch.

column5: For monitoring oil temperature during infusion and EO addition (must be below 40°C for EO addition).
column6: Digital probe thermometer. Available at any kitchen supply store.

column5: 50ml, 100ml, 500ml, 1L graduated cylinders. SS funnel set (3 sizes). For accurate measurement and filling.
column6: Plastic measuring tools interact with EOs. Use only SS or borosilicate glass.

column4: Procure SS ladles, spoons, mesh strainer set
column5: For stirring, scooping, and filtering herb material from oil after infusion.
column6: Fine mesh SS strainer is critical for filtering herb residue from finished oil.

column4: Procure nitrile gloves, masks, aprons, hair nets
column5: PPE for all manufacturing activities. Minimum 500 pairs gloves, 50 aprons, 100 hair nets.
column6: Inspector checks PPE availability. Also good practice.

column4: Procure SS shelving for raw material storage
column5: Wall-mounted or floor SS shelving for ingredient storage. Separate from packaging storage.
column6: Do not store raw materials on the floor or in cardboard boxes directly.

column5: Printed labels for each shelf zone: ingredient name, date received, batch, FIFO indicators.
column6: Inspectors look for organised, labelled storage.

column4: Engage regulatory consultant for licence application
column5: Find a regulatory consultant familiar with TN Drug Controller process. Fee: ₹8,000–₹15,000 for application preparation and follow-up.
column6: First-time applicants almost always make mistakes without a consultant. Money well spent.

column5: Documents needed: LLP COI, PAN, GST certificate, LLP agreement, premises photos + layout plan, equipment list, QP credentials + letter, product list, SOP drafts.
column6: Consultant will guide checklist. Prepare everything before submitting.

column4: Submit cosmetic manufacturing licence application to TN Drug Controller
column5: Submit to Assistant Drug Controller, Tamil Nadu. Pay application fee. Receive acknowledgement.
column6: Application fee: ₹2,000–₹5,000. After submission: wait for inspector visit scheduling (typically 3–8 weeks).

column5: Clean room thoroughly. Have all equipment in place and labelled. Have QP present. Have printed SOPs displayed. Have BMR templates ready to show.
column6: Inspector may visit 3–8 weeks after application. Be ready from Day 42 onwards.

column5: Cover: ₹10–₹25 lakh. Protects against consumer claims for adverse reactions. Get quotes from: Bajaj Allianz, HDFC Ergo, New India Assurance.
column6: Get this before first sale. Annual premium is modest relative to downside risk.

column5: Zoho Books (₹7,200/year) or Tally Prime (₹18,000/year). Set up: chart of accounts, product codes, GST rates (18%), bank feed.
column6: Start from Day 1 of the LLP. Every rupee in and out must be recorded from the beginning.

column4: Lock Master Formula Record (MFR) — Hair Oil
column5: Document precisely: every ingredient by INCI name, grams per 10L batch, % of formula, processing group (fresh-cooked / dry-infused / EO blend), addition sequence, target temperature at each stage. Sign and date.
column6: This is the legal manufacturing record. Do not alter without formal review and new version number. Store physical signed copy in locked file.

column5: Sheet tracking: version number, date, what changed, why, who approved, first batch applied to. Even if only one version exists, start the log now.
column6: Protects formula integrity. Prevents well-meaning people from 'simplifying' the formula without a formal decision.

column4: Lock MFR — Heart Chakra Bath Salt
column5: Document: Epsom salt quantity, Himalayan salt quantity, essential oils (bergamot, rose) in grams not drops, Polysorbate 20 quantity, dried rose petals quantity. Note: Polysorbate 20 is critical for oil-in-water dispersion — must be documented.
column6: Bath salt appears simple but oil separation in water is a common failure. Polysorbate 20 ratio must be precise.

column4: Lock MFR — Root Chakra Bath Salt
column5: Same format as Heart Chakra. Document frankincense and lavender EO quantities precisely. Note dried flower quantities.
column6: Keep both bath salt MFRs in same format for consistency.

column5: Step-by-step from sanitisation through to final filling. Must cover: fresh ingredient cooking and moisture evaporation process, dry herb infusion (time, temperature, filtration), EO addition below 40°C, QC checks at each stage. Print, laminate, post on wall.
column6: This is the most complex SOP. Allocate 1 full day to write it properly.

column5: Dedicated SOP for fresh botanicals: onion, ginger, curry leaves, hibiscus, aloe vera, basil, mint. Covers: washing, chopping, quantity, cooking in oil, moisture evaporation signs, when to proceed to next step. This is the most technically specific SOP.
column6: Fresh ingredient processing is where most handmade oils have quality inconsistency. This SOP is your quality differentiator.

column5: Covers: cold infusion vs warm infusion decision, temperature range, duration, filtration process, acceptable colour and odour outcomes, rejection criteria.
column6: Separate from fresh ingredient SOP. Different process, different risks.

column5: Temperature requirement (below 40°C — non-negotiable), sequence of EO addition, blending time, why this temperature matters. Post in large print near the SS vessel.
column6: EO degradation above 40°C destroys fragrance and some therapeutic properties. This SOP prevents the most common handmade oil mistake.

column5: Polysorbate 20 mixing with EOs first, then addition to salt, mixing sequence, dried flower addition, jar filling, sealing. QC checks for: fragrance intensity, visual appearance, no oil separation test.
column6: Simpler than hair oil but still needs documentation for consistency.

column5: QC checkpoints: after filtration, pre-fill, post-fill. What to check at each: colour vs reference standard, odour, pH, fill volume (100ml ± 2ml), viscosity observation, cap torque.
column6: Create a colour reference card from first approved batch. Every subsequent batch compared against it.

column4: Write SOP-QC-002: Incoming Raw Material Check
column5: For every delivery: visual inspection, odour check, weight verification vs invoice, supplier batch number recorded. Acceptance / rejection criteria. Return process.
column6: Single most important contamination prevention step.

column4: Write SOP-PKG-001: Bottle Filling and Capping
column5: Fill weight per bottle, fill tool, wipe exterior, nozzle cap application, torque check, leak test (invert 30 seconds). Comb cap separate pouch into carton.
column6: Leaking bottles are the fastest way to destroy brand trust.

column5: Label jig use, correct orientation, centre alignment, batch stamp position, bubble removal. Crooked labels on a Rs 599 product are unacceptable.
column6: Build a simple cardboard jig to ensure every label is placed identically.

column4: Write SOP-PKG-003: Carton Assembly and Dispatch
column5: Carton fold, insert card placement, product placement, seal, batch number on carton, pre-dispatch QC checklist, stacking for storage.
column6: Include visual guide with photos if possible.

column5: Fire extinguisher location, EO flash point awareness, no naked flames near EOs, PPE requirements, first aid location, emergency contacts list posted on wall.
column6: Post on wall in manufacturing room permanently.

column5: Template fields: batch number (format: SVHOP-YYYYMMDD-001), product, date, operator name, each ingredient quantity in (weighed and recorded), any deviations, QC check results at each stage, yield, packaging quantity, QP sign-off. Print 50 copies.
column6: One BMR per batch. File all BMRs for 5 years. This is your legal protection.

column5: Fields: batch number, product, date, quantity retained (50ml minimum), storage location, monthly observation notes, disposal date.
column6: Keep one retain sample per batch for 2 years (or product shelf life + 6 months).

column4: Calculate precise COGS for hair oil (100ml)
column5: From MFR quantities: total ingredient cost per 10L batch ÷ yield = per 100ml cost. Add: bottle + cap + label + carton + insert + labour (allocated) + overhead (allocated). Target: below ₹140 per unit.
column6: If COGS exceeds ₹140, review packaging costs first, not ingredient quantities.

column4: Calculate COGS for each bath salt SKU (150g)
column5: Same process. Target total COGS below ₹65 per unit for ₹299 MRP to yield acceptable margin.
column6: Bath salts have simpler formula. COGS should be well under ₹65.

column4: Build vendor master for all 61 ingredients
column5: For each ingredient: vendor name, location, contact, MOQ, price per kg, lead time, quality notes, backup vendor identified. Use Supply Chain sheet.
column6: Do this before placing first bulk order. Quality varies enormously between vendors.

column4: Request samples from 2 vendors per Tier 1 ingredient
column5: Tier 1 ingredients (>5% of formula by volume): coconut oil, sesame oil, jojoba oil. Order 100ml–500ml samples from 2 different vendors each. Evaluate: colour, odour, viscosity, clarity.
column6: Never bulk order without sample evaluation first.

column4: Source essential oils from Kannauj vendors
column5: Contact 2–3 Kannauj (UP) essential oil suppliers: SN Perfumeries, Shakti EO, others via IndiaMART. Request samples of: frankincense, bergamot, lavender, patchouli, frankincense. MOQ typically 100g–500g.
column6: Kannauj is India's EO capital. Quality and price far better than retail Chennai. Shipping 2–3 days.

column4: Source rare Tamil herbs from Chennai Siddha suppliers
column5: Magizhmpoo (Spanish Cherry Flower), Anjanakal (Surma Stone), Karboga Arisi (Babchi), Korai Kilangu (Nut Grass), Ponnanganni: source from Siddha medicine suppliers on Chennai Bypass Road / Nattar Pettai area.
column6: These are your brand differentiators. Do not substitute with generic alternatives.

column4: Buy buffer stock of seasonal ingredients
column5: Amla (season Oct–Feb), Spanish Cherry Flower (seasonal), Rose Petals (season Feb–Apr): buy dried 6-month supply when available. Do not wait until needed.
column6: Seasonal stockouts will interrupt production. Buy ahead.

column5: SS shelves labelled by ingredient. FIFO labels on every container. EOs in cool dark location (AC room or insulated box). Fresh botanicals: refrigerator. Dried herbs: airtight bins.
column6: Temperature matters for EO quality. Chennai heat will degrade EOs stored at room temperature.

column5: Airtight SS or food-grade HDPE containers for dried herbs (500g, 1kg, 5kg sizes). Dark glass bottles for EOs. Food-grade drums (5L, 20L) for base oils.
column6: Label every container: ingredient, vendor, date received, batch number.

column4: Set up raw material inventory tracker
column5: Google Sheet or Zoho Inventory: ingredient name, unit, quantity on hand, reorder point, reorder quantity, supplier, last purchase date, price.
column6: Update every time ingredients are received or used in a batch.

column4: Deploy existing 1,100 PET bottles for beta and first orders
column5: Transparent PET is suitable for beta testing and early gifting. Test leak performance with nozzle cap first. Deploy this inventory before ordering new bottles.
column6: This is ₹15,000–₹22,000 of sunk investment. Extract full value from it.

column4: Test PET bottle with nozzle cap for leak performance
column5: Fill 10 bottles with actual hair oil. Apply nozzle cap. Invert for 24 hours at room temperature and 40°C (simulate Chennai summer transit). Check for any leakage.
column6: This test must happen before any orders are dispatched. Leaking bottles = returns and brand damage.

column4: Source amber PET 100ml bottles (500 units) for commercial launch
column5: Vendors: SB Packaging Chennai, Maharashtra packaging belt (IndiaMART search: amber PET 100ml pump bottle). Get samples first. Check: neck diameter fits nozzle/pump cap, base flat, label area dimensions match label design.
column6: Order samples before bulk. Neck diameter is critical — must match cap spec.

column5: Match neck diameter of chosen bottle. Test with actual oil: pump must dispense 1–2ml per press. Metal actuator preferred for premium feel.
column6: Test cap on bottle with actual oil before ordering bulk. Oil viscosity affects pump performance.

column5: Small resealable polybag or kraft envelope to contain comb cap inside carton. Print: 'Comb Applicator — For Scalp Application.' This adds perceived value and solves the transit leak problem.
column6: Comb cap in branded mini-pouch inside carton = premium unboxing detail.

column4: Finalise label content for all three panels
column5: Front panel: confirmed. Left panel (QR side): QR code + MRP + MFG date field + expiry field + address + licence number field. Right panel (benefit side): replace Lorem ipsum with three claim sections — 'Natural Scent', 'Natural Ingredients', 'No Synthetic Fragrance' — write 2 sentences each aligned to Food For Hair positioning.
column6: Right panel content must be written before label can go to print. Three 2-sentence descriptions needed.

column4: Add compliance fields to label artwork
column5: Ensure label includes: INCI ingredient list (all 61+ in descending order by weight), net quantity (100ml), batch number field (blank for stamping), MFG date field, best before field, MRP (₹599 or revised), manufacturer name + address + licence number field, directions, cautions.
column6: Get label proof reviewed by regulatory consultant before printing. Cost: ₹2,000–₹3,000 for review.

column5: Register at gs1india.org. Get GTIN (barcode number) for each SKU. Mandatory for retail — POS cannot scan without barcode.
column6: Without barcode: retail chain cannot process your product. Register all 3 launch SKUs simultaneously.

column5: Embed EAN-13 barcode in back panel of label. Print test barcode and scan with barcode scanner app to verify it reads correctly.
column6: Do not print final labels until barcode scan test passes.

column4: Print first label run (1,000 per SKU)
column5: Print on matte weatherproof adhesive stock. Test adhesion on PET and amber PET bottles. Test heat resistance (labels must not peel in Chennai summer at 40°C+ in transit).
column6: Get printed samples before full run. Check: colours match screen, text legible at 1.5mm minimum font size.

column5: Matchbox-sleeve style (confirmed direction from Gemini / ChatGPT). Kraft board 300 gsm. External: logo, product name, 61+, key benefit line. Internal top flap: 2-line ingredient story + QR code + 'Handmade in Chennai'. Bottom: batch number area.
column6: The sleeve/carton is premium-signal 1. Get it right.

column5: Get 3 quotes from Chennai packaging vendors. Check: print quality on kraft, box rigidity, fit of bottle inside sleeve. Order 500 units for launch — do not over-order on first print run.
column6: Packaging vendors: check Saidapet area and Guindy industrial estate in Chennai.

column4: Design and print product insert card
column5: 90mm × 60mm or similar. Side 1: Svaas origin story (100 words, Food For Hair philosophy). Side 2: how to use + 5 hero ingredients (Bhringraj, Amla, Jatamasi, Coconut, Frankincense) + WhatsApp number + QR code for ingredient library.
column6: Print on recycled / seed paper. This is a brand-building touchpoint read at first use.

column4: Design gift box for 2-product set (hair oil + 1 bath salt)
column5: Kraft gift box with pull ribbon, tissue paper, personalised card slot. Can be used for Diwali / New Year / Women's Day corporate gifting. MOQ 100 units.
column6: Gift box = immediate corporate revenue opportunity. Design it before soft launch.

column5: Simple acrylic or cardboard jig marking exact label position on bottle. Ensures every bottle has label in same position. Prevents the crooked-label problem.
column6: This takes 30 minutes to make and saves hours of rework. Do it before first labelling session.

column5: Define in one locked document: What Svaas is. What Svaas is not. The Food For Hair philosophy. Tone of voice. What claims are never made. Visual principles. What the brand will never become. Every agency, designer, and content creator works from this document.
column6: This is the most important brand document. Without it, every external contributor will interpret the brand differently.

column4: Rewrite all existing content for Food For Hair positioning
column5: Audit: 30 social posts, website copy, ingredient descriptions, product descriptions. Replace: 'hair fall treatment' language with 'nourishment' language. Replace: 'hair growth' with 'supports healthy hair growth'. Remove all therapeutic claim language before any asset goes live.
column6: This is a focused 2–3 day content project. Must happen before any marketing begins.

column5: Plan: Shopify Basic (₹1,499/month). Theme: Dawn or Debut. Domain: purchased domain. Integrate: Razorpay (2% TDR). Install: Loox for reviews, Klaviyo for email.
column6: Build the store now so it can be tested before soft launch. A live but not-yet-public store.

column4: Create product pages — all 3 launch SKUs
column5: Each page: 5+ product photos, product name, price, 3 key claims (bullets), ingredient highlights, how to use, FAQ section, reviews placeholder. Mobile-first layout.
column6: Most customers will view on mobile. Test every page on mobile before launch.

column5: Use Svaaskriti_Ingredients.docx as source. Create one card per ingredient: English name + Tamil name + Sanskrit name where available + benefit description + hero ingredient badge for top 10. Link from product page and QR code.
column6: This is the proof behind 61+. It is the most powerful trust-building page on the site.

column5: Create Razorpay account. Link to business current account. Integrate with Shopify. Test with ₹1 transaction. Confirm KYC completion (required before large payouts).
column6: Do not go live without testing a real payment end-to-end.

column5: Create Shiprocket account. Integrate with Shopify. Set shipping rules: free above ₹999, ₹80 flat below. Set courier preference: Delhivery (sub-500g). Test label generation.
column6: Shiprocket automates label printing and courier booking. Critical for order fulfilment.

column5: Install GA4 tag. Install Meta Pixel. Set up conversion events: Add to Cart, Checkout Initiated, Purchase. Verify data is flowing before launch.
column6: Data from Day 1 of launch. Cannot be retrofitted.

column4: Shoot hero product photography — 30 images minimum
column5: Styles needed: (a) Clean marble flat-lay × 10 (b) Nature/herb-immersed × 10 (c) In-use / hands applying oil × 5 (d) Gifting arrangement × 5. Use terracotta packaging direction. Amber bottle.
column6: Shoot with prototype packaging if final not ready. Can reshoot with final packaging later. Do not delay all marketing waiting for perfect final packaging.

column5: Sequence: herbs in Chennai market → weighing on scale → oil being processed → woman at window applying oil → she breathes slowly. No dialogue. Music only. This is the anchor launch content piece.
column6: Shoot in Oodles office / available natural-light space. Does not need a film crew — a good videographer with a DSLR is sufficient.

column5: Username: consistent with brand name. Bio: 60 characters — 'Ritual wellness. Handmade in Chennai.' Link to Shopify via Linktree or direct.
column6: Set up now even if posting starts in Phase 4. Reserve the handle.

column4: Plan first 30 days of Instagram content
column5: Map 3 posts/week × 4 weeks = 12 posts. Post types: 4 ingredient carousels, 3 product flat-lays, 2 how-to reels, 2 founder story posts, 1 launch announcement. Create all 12 before launch week.
column6: Creating content in real-time during launch week is chaotic. Build the buffer before launch.

column5: Free up to 250 contacts. Configure: welcome series (3 emails), post-purchase Day 7 / Day 30 / Day 45, abandoned cart 60 min / 24 hr / 72 hr, birthday campaign.
column6: Write all email copy before launch. Do not wing it after orders start coming in.

column5: Business name, description, catalogue with 3 products, quick reply scripts for: order status, ingredients query, return request. Separate business number from personal.
column6: WhatsApp will be your highest-conversion early channel. Set it up properly.

column4: Send hair oil sample for microbial testing
column5: Submit 200ml of finished oil from first batch to NABL-accredited lab. Test: Total Plate Count, Yeast and Mould, E. coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
column6: Labs in Chennai: SGS India (Guindy), Intertek, TUV SUD. Lead time: 5–7 working days.

column4: Send hair oil for heavy metals testing
column5: Test: arsenic, lead, mercury, cadmium. Same lab as microbial. Send separately or as combined panel if lab offers it.
column6: Essential oils and herbal extracts can carry trace metals. This is non-negotiable for consumer safety.

column4: Begin accelerated stability testing — hair oil
column5: Set up 5 samples at: 40°C/75% humidity (stress), 25°C/60% humidity (controlled), ambient room temp. Observe monthly: colour, odour, viscosity, pH, any separation. Run for 3 months minimum to support 18-month shelf life claim on label.
column6: Buy a small incubator if needed (₹5,000–₹8,000). Alternatively use a warm enclosed space. Monthly observations documented in M07 Retain Sample Log.

column4: Send bath salt samples for microbial testing
column5: Both Heart Chakra and Root Chakra variants. Same lab.
column6: Bath salts with dried flowers can have microbial contamination from plant material.

column5: Ask 10 people to smell the hair oil and score fragrance 1–10. Ask: what notes do you detect? What does it remind you of? Is it pleasant? Would the scent put you off purchasing?
column6: The exhibition feedback included 'didn't smell good.' This must be measured formally before launch. If average score below 6: review EO ratios.

column4: If fragrance score below 6: adjust EO ratios
column5: Increase: bergamot, vanilla, lavender (fragrance-forward EOs already in formula). Reduce: patchouli, vetiver dominance. Do not add synthetic fragrance. Re-evaluate with 10 people.
column6: This is the one formula change that may be genuinely needed. Fragrance is part of the ritual experience.

column5: Criteria: 5 oily scalp, 5 dry scalp, 5 normal. Mix of: friends, family, domestic staff, students, neighbours. Provide free 100ml bottle. Brief them: this is 4-week trial, please use 3×/week minimum.
column6: Diverse hair types give more representative data than a homogeneous group.

column4: Create Google Form — hair oil feedback
column5: Fields: hair type, scalp type, frequency of use, benefits observed (multi-select), issues observed, fragrance rating 1–5, texture rating 1–5, cooling sensation (yes/no), before/after photo upload, NPS 1–10, price willing to pay. Under 10 questions total.
column6: Keep the form short. Long forms = low completion. Link sent via WhatsApp.

column4: Create WhatsApp group for beta testers
column5: Group for real-time feedback. Post weekly prompts: 'What do you notice after Week 1?' Share usage tips. Respond to every message. This group becomes your first community.
column6: Real-time feedback is more valuable than forms alone. The WhatsApp group is also your first brand community.

column5: Weekly WhatsApp message to each tester: 1 question, 1 photo request. Keep it light and conversational. Do not ask for reviews yet.
column6: Track responses in a simple Google Sheet.

column5: Different people from hair oil group if possible. Give both Heart Chakra and Root Chakra. Track: dissolution, fragrance, skin feel, relaxation effect, colour staining (critical — no staining acceptable).
column6: Any colour staining means dye must be removed or changed to cosmetic-grade before launch.

column5: Include: varying hair lengths, colour-treated hair, post-partum hair loss, oily scalp, Type 4 curl pattern, men with scalp issues. Source via Instagram stories + WhatsApp broadcast.
column6: Broader testing catches edge cases that inner circle cannot.

column5: Briefing: Week 0 photo (clean hair, natural light) + Week 4 photo (same conditions). Request WhatsApp consent for use in marketing.
column6: Even 5 compelling before/afters are powerful launch content. 25+ is excellent.

column4: Identify 5 pilot salons in Chennai for product seeding
column5: Target: premium wellness/natural-oriented salons in Nungambakkam, Alwarpet, Adyar, RA Puram. Look for salons with Ayurvedic or natural positioning. Instagram search: #chennaihairsalon #ayurvedictreatment.
column6: Salons are recommendation channels first, not sales channels. They give credibility.

column4: Visit 5 salons — do not pitch, observe first
column5: Visit as a customer. Note: existing product range, price points, staff knowledge, client profile, what Ayurvedic products they use. Only after observation: introduce yourself and the product.
column6: Do not walk in with a pitch. Build a relationship first.

column4: Gift free product to 5 salons for professional trial
column5: Give 2 × 100ml bottles to each salon owner / head stylist. Ask for honest professional feedback after 2 weeks of use.
column6: No strings. No purchase request. This is a credibility-building investment.

column5: Written protocol: if any tester reports rash, swelling, or irritation: (1) Stop supply from that batch, (2) Get full details and batch number, (3) Pull retain sample, (4) Advise tester to see a doctor (at Svaas cost if needed), (5) Consult QP and regulatory consultant, (6) Full refund.
column6: This must be written before testing begins, not after an event occurs.

column5: Minimum criteria to launch: (a) NPS average ≥7 (b) ≥75% positive feedback (c) ≥3 beta testers actively requesting to reorder (d) 0 serious adverse events (e) Microbial and heavy metals tests passed (f) Stability stable at 8 weeks. All 6 must be met.
column6: Hard stop. Do not launch if any criterion is not met.

column5: Buy: Indulekha Bringha Oil, Kama Ayurveda Bringadi Oil, Biotique Bio Bhringraj, Mamaearth Onion Hair Oil, WOW Onion Oil. Use each for 1 week. Note: fragrance, texture, absorption, packaging experience, price per ml. Document honestly.
column6: You must know your competition from personal experience, not just research.

column5: For each competitor: price, size, price-per-ml, key claims, key ingredients, packaging quality, Instagram following, review count, common customer complaints (Amazon/Nykaa reviews are goldmine).
column6: Customer complaints on competitor Amazon reviews = your positioning opportunities.

column5: Written process: orders pulled by 11am, packed by 2pm, courier collected by 4pm, tracking uploaded, customer WhatsApp sent. Post on wall.
column6: Consistency builds trust. Late shipments destroy early brand goodwill.

column5: Step-by-step: verify product and qty, wrap in tissue, place insert card, seal carton, attach shipping label, note batch number in dispatch log.
column6: Batch number in dispatch log = traceability if a complaint comes in.

column4: Stock first commercial inventory (200 units hair oil)
column5: Manufacture and pack 200 units of hair oil for soft launch inventory. Use existing PET bottles with printed labels (including batch number).
column6: 200 units supports 6–8 weeks of initial orders at 25–30 units/week.

column4: Stock first commercial inventory (100 units each bath salt)
column5: Manufacture and pack 100 units each of Heart and Root Chakra bath salts.
column6: Bath salts are simpler to produce. Can be made in 2–3 hours per 100-unit batch.

column5: Dedicated area in manufacturing room or adjacent space. Shelves labelled by SKU and batch. Away from direct light and heat. Track units in FG inventory sheet.
column6: Never store finished goods on the floor.

column5: Place a test order on Shopify from a personal device. Pay via Razorpay. Pack the order. Generate Shiprocket label. Dispatch. Receive. Check product condition on arrival.
column6: This end-to-end test reveals every gap in the fulfilment process before real customers experience it.

column4: Announce on WhatsApp — inner circle (100 people)
column5: Send personal WhatsApp message to 100 known contacts: 'We are launching Svaas Svaaskriti Hair Oil in a few weeks. You get first access. Reply if you want to be on the early list.' No link yet.
column6: Building a waitlist before launch creates launch-day demand. Aim for 30–50 confirmed interest responses.

column5: Post 1: Founder story — why Svaas exists. Post 2: 61 ingredients flat-lay with ingredient names. Post 3: behind-the-scenes of oil being made. No product sale link yet. Just community building.
column6: Pre-launch posts with no selling pressure get higher organic engagement.

column5: Identify 15 Chennai wellness/beauty nano-influencers (5K–50K followers, >3% engagement). DM personally. Gift product. Ask for honest review if they love it — no payment required initially.
column6: Nano-influencers in Chennai have high local trust. One honest post from a credible account is worth 10 paid posts.

column5: 1-page PDF: gift set options (₹599 / ₹999 / ₹1,499 / ₹1,999), customisation options, MOQ 25 units, bulk pricing, lead time 7–10 days, contact details. Clean, professional design — not a brochure.
column6: Corporate gifting is the fastest early revenue channel if you have network access.

column4: Build list of 25 Chennai corporate prospects
column5: HR/Admin contacts at IT companies, consulting firms, pharma, financial services. Diwali / Pongal / Women's Day are primary gifting occasions. Decision made 4–6 weeks before festival.
column6: LinkedIn is the best tool for finding HR/Admin contacts.

column5: Contents: printed brand story (1 page), product description, 2 product photos on USB / email, QR code to ingredient library, handwritten note from founder. 10 kits.
column6: Send to: Vogue India (beauty editor), Femina, The Hindu MetroPlus, Behindwoods lifestyle, GoodHomes, Verve.

column5: Documents ready: company profile, GSTIN certificate, cosmetic licence copy (when received), product catalogue, MRP and trade prices (MRP minus 35%), barcode list, test reports summary, label compliance declaration.
column6: Do not approach retail chain buyer without ALL of this ready.

column4: Schedule informal meeting with retail chain contact
column5: Not a pitch meeting. A 'getting to know your requirements' meeting. Questions to ask: What is their customer profile? What natural products do they currently carry? What is their margin expectation? What is their vendor onboarding process?
column6: Learn before pitching. Never pitch on the first meeting.

column5: Build a 1-page comparison: competitor prices per 100ml (Indulekha, Kama, Biotique, WOW, Mamaearth). Calculate Svaas price per ml at ₹499 / ₹599 / ₹699 / ₹799. Survey 20 target customers: show product and ask what price they would expect. Decide MRP before labels print.
column6: Price is very hard to increase post-launch. Decide once, decide with data.

column4: Open Shopify to first 50 orders — WhatsApp only
column5: Share Shopify link via personal WhatsApp only. Offer: ₹50 off first order (code: SVAAS50) in exchange for a photo review. Goal: 50 orders in first 2 weeks.
column6: Soft launch = learning, not revenue maximisation. Fix every fulfilment issue before going public.

column4: Post launch day content on Instagram
column5: Post 1 (launch day): Brand film reel. Post 2: Founder story carousel. Post 3: Three before/after testimonials from beta testers. Stories: behind-the-scenes of packing first orders.
column6: Coordinate all three posts on same day. Launch day = maximum organic reach.

column4: Personal message to every first 50 customers
column5: After each of first 50 orders: send personal WhatsApp from founder: 'Thank you for being one of our first 50. Your order is packed with care.' Include: expected delivery date.
column6: Personal touches in the first 50 orders create disproportionate loyalty and word-of-mouth.

column5: Ask first 10 buyers to send an unboxing photo. Check: product condition, packaging integrity, insert card present, no leaks. Identify any fulfilment issues.
column6: Fix any issue before the next 40 orders. Do not scale broken fulfilment.

column5: Send email + WhatsApp to HR contacts at 5 Chennai companies. Attach gifting catalogue PDF. Offer: ₹99 sample set (deducted from first order). Diwali timing if in Oct–Nov. Women's Day if in Feb–Mar.
column6: Make trial barrier as low as possible.

column5: Every morning: (a) Check Shopify for new orders (b) Check Shiprocket for dispatch status (c) Check WhatsApp for customer messages (d) Update FG inventory count (e) Flag any stock approaching reorder point.
column6: This 15-minute morning ritual prevents every operational surprise.

column5: Policy: accept returns for damaged/wrong product within 7 days. No return on opened product except safety concerns. Process: customer sends photo → verify → send replacement or refund within 48 hours.
column6: How you handle the first return defines your brand more than how you handle the first sale.

column4: Tag all first 50 customers in Shopify
column5: Tag: 'Founding Customer'. These customers get: early access to new products, personal notes from founder, gratitude discount on second order. Treat them as VIPs permanently.
column6: Founding customers who feel special become brand advocates. Their word-of-mouth is worth more than advertising.

column5: Klaviyo email Day 7 after delivery: 3-question mini-survey — How is your hair feeling? What have you noticed? Anything we should know? Reply-friendly, not a formal survey.
column6: Day 7 is peak engagement. Customer has used the product enough to form an opinion.

column4: Confirm licence received before public launch
column5: Do not run public paid advertising or approach media before cosmetic manufacturing licence is in hand. Soft launch to personal network is acceptable without licence. Public commercial launch requires it.
column6: This is the line. Personal network = soft launch OK. Strangers = need licence.

column5: Once licence is received, add licence number to label artwork. Reprint labels. Apply to all units before public launch.
column6: Labels without licence number are non-compliant for commercial sale.

column5: First paid campaign: test 3 creative concepts (brand film / before-after / ingredient story). Target: Chennai + Bangalore + Mumbai women 22–40, interests: wellness, Ayurveda, natural beauty.
column6: Start small. Optimise ROAS before scaling budget.

column4: Submit PR kits to 10 media contacts
column5: Send kits with personalised note from founder. Follow up after 1 week with a brief WhatsApp message.
column6: PR coverage is a long-shot but the backlinks and credibility are worth the cost of the kits.

column4: Make formal retail pitch to chain buyer
column5: Now you have: licence, barcode, retail-grade packaging, test reports, 50+ orders of proof. Bring: product samples, gifting catalogue, trade price list, onboarding documentation.
column6: This meeting is the culmination of 5 months of preparation. Be ready.

column5: When orders exceed 100/month: hire 1 part-time helper (2–3 days/week) for: labelling, carton assembly, ingredient weighing, cleaning. Train to all relevant SOPs before first unsupervised session.
column6: Free founder from packing to focus on brand and sales.

column5: Connect to Shopify. Automate: order confirmation, shipping update, delivery confirmation, Day-3 usage tip, Day-7 feedback request.
column6: AiSensy starter: ₹999/month. Worth it from Month 6 when manual messages become unmanageable.

column5: Simple mechanic: give customer a unique code. When a friend orders using the code: ₹50 off for friend, ₹50 credit for referrer. Track in Shopify.
column6: Referral CAC is effectively zero. Works best when product quality drives genuine recommendation.

column5: Once retail chain agrees: supply first stock (consignment or sale-or-return for pilot, hard sell for all subsequent orders), provide display unit, brief store staff on product, set replenishment schedule.
column6: Display unit at counter = 30–40% higher sell-through rate vs shelf stacking.

column4: Evaluate launching remaining 5 chakra bath salts
column5: Decision gate: if Heart + Root Chakra have achieved 50+ units sold each: manufacture Solar Plexus, Sacral, Throat, Third Eye, Crown variants. If not: hold and focus on hero SKU.
column6: Only expand when current SKUs have velocity.

column5: Full P&L review with CA. Update unit economics (actual COGS vs estimate). Recalculate break-even. Update 6-month cash flow forecast. Assess: is pricing right? Is any channel unprofitable?
column6: Monthly reviews prevent financial surprises.

column4: Upgrade to second 20L vessel + capacity review
column5: When orders consistently exceed 300/month: add second 20L vessel. New capacity: ~400 units/week. Review if 150 sq ft is sufficient for this volume.
column6: Plan capacity upgrade 2 months before hitting ceiling. Never scramble.

column5: Apply to Nykaa marketplace if: (a) 100+ genuine website reviews, (b) consistent supply available, (c) brand positioning is established. Commission: 18–25%.
column6: Nykaa brings discovery but erodes brand positioning if entered too early.

column5: For 5 pilot salons with positive feedback: introduce 200ml professional SKU. Pricing: ₹450 for 200ml (wholesale). Salons charge ₹300–₹600 for scalp treatment using product. Salon ROI is strong.
column6: The math must work for the salon, not just for Svaas.

column4: Launch ingredient spotlight series — 1 per week
column5: Post one ingredient deep-dive per week: ingredient name in English + Tamil + Sanskrit, what it does for hair, where it comes from, why it is in Svaaskriti. 57 ingredients = 57 weeks of content.
column6: This series educates customers and creates SEO-rich content simultaneously.

column5: Article 1: 'Why Ayurvedic Hair Oiling Works.' Article 2: 'The Science Behind 61 Ingredients.' Article 3: 'What is Hair Nourishment vs Hair Treatment?' Article 4: 'How to Build a Hair Ritual.' Target: 1,500 words each. Keyword-rich.
column6: Long-form SEO content compounds over time. Start now for Year 2 organic benefit.

column5: When Instagram exceeds 5K followers and orders consistently above 300/month: hire 1 part-time or full-time marketing executive. Role: content creation, community management, influencer coordination. Range: ₹18,000–₹28,000/month.
column6: Do not hire before revenue supports the cost.

column5: When monthly revenue consistently exceeds ₹5 lakh: evaluate renting 500–800 sq ft dedicated manufacturing unit. Separate from Oodles office. Allows larger batch processing and cleaner compliance.
column6: Do not move before revenue justifies it. 150 sq ft + Oodles office can support up to ~500 units/month.

column5: Required for: export, major retail chains, Nykaa premium listing. Cost: ₹50,000–₹1,50,000. Timeline: 3–6 months. Start process in Year 2.
column6: GMP certification is a Year 2 investment that opens Year 3 opportunities.

column5: Apply online at DGFT portal. Fee: ₹500. Needed before any export. Can be done any time — file as soon as business is established.
column6: Takes 2–3 days. No reason to delay.

column5: After 100+ reviews on own website, consistent supply, and established brand: apply to Nykaa seller programme. Prepare: brand story, licence copy, test reports, white-background product photography, Nykaa-format product descriptions.
column6: Nykaa brings discovery but high commission. Plan economics before listing.

column4: Launch soap range (Cinn-Oat Glow, Wine Elegance)
column5: After hair oil has velocity: evaluate soap launch. Soaps require: cold-process SOP, lye handling safety protocol, cure time (4–6 weeks), separate packaging. Defer until Year 2.
column6: Do not rush soaps. They require a genuinely separate manufacturing process.

column5: When orders consistently exceed 1,000/month: hire dedicated operations manager. Role: batch scheduling, vendor management, QC oversight, shipping management. Range: ₹25,000–₹40,000/month.
column6: Operations manager frees founder from daily ops and enables scale.

column5: Every month: plan batches based on sales forecast + 6-week buffer. Schedule batch days. Prepare ingredients list. Execute with BMR documentation.
column6: Never manufacture without a written batch plan and completed BMR.

column5: Every Monday: physical count of all SKUs. Compare to Shopify inventory. Reconcile discrepancies. Flag any SKU below 6-week buffer.
column6: Shopify inventory is not always accurate. Physical count is the truth.

column5: Every Monday: review RM inventory tracker. Any ingredient below reorder point: trigger purchase order that day.
column6: Ingredient stockouts stop production. Catch them a month early, not a day late.

column5: By 5th of each month: reconcile previous month revenue, COGS, operating expenses. Update rolling cash flow forecast. Share with CA for GST filing inputs.
column6: Monthly close is non-negotiable. It is how you know if the business is healthy.

column5: GSTR-1 by 11th of each month. GSTR-3B by 20th. Input credit reconciliation with GSTR-2B monthly.
column6: Missed GST filing attracts penalties. Set calendar reminders.

column5: Maintain content calendar. Do not go silent for more than 3 days. Mix: ingredient education, product, ritual inspiration, customer stories, founder perspective.
column6: Consistency compounds. 3 posts per week × 52 weeks = 156 touchpoints per year.

column5: Themes: one ingredient story, one ritual idea, one behind-the-scenes, one product update. Under 300 words. Personal tone.
column6: Email newsletter has the highest ROI of any marketing channel for small brands.

column5: Klaviyo: email Day 10 post-delivery requesting review with photo. WhatsApp Day 10 for customers who have not opened email.
column6: Reviews compound. 100 reviews = significantly higher conversion rate.

column4: Monitor and respond to all complaints within 24 hours
column5: Check: Shopify reviews, Instagram comments, WhatsApp, email. Every complaint gets a personal response within 24 hours regardless of platform.
column6: One unresolved public complaint costs more than 10 good reviews.

column5: First Friday of every month: review previous month metrics — revenue, units, complaints, NPS, top feedback themes. Update decision log. Set 3 priorities for next month.
column6: This ritual keeps strategy alive in the middle of daily operations.

column5: Every 3 months: review business against milestone map. Assess: product market fit, channel performance, team needs, upcoming compliance renewals, product roadmap progress.
column6: Quarterly review prevents drift. Forces honest assessment of what is working and what is not.

column5: Check: (a) Licence current? (b) BMRs filed for all batches? (c) Any complaints flagged to regulatory? (d) Label changes needed? (e) Test reports up to date?
column6: 15 minutes per month. Non-compliance compounds silently.

column5: For each vendor: quality score, delivery reliability, price vs market, any issues. Decide: retain / renegotiate / replace.
column6: Vendor quality drifts if not reviewed. A bad batch of a key ingredient ruins production.

column5: Open a separate savings account linked to current account for: tax provisions, working capital reserve. Transfer 15% of each payment received into savings for GST liability and contingency.
column6: Separating tax money prevents the common mistake of spending GST collected before filing date.

column4: Create chart of accounts in Zoho Books
column5: Set up accounts: Revenue (D2C / Retail / Corporate / Salon), COGS (Ingredients / Packaging / Labour / Overhead), OpEx (Rent / Marketing / Compliance / Logistics / People / Misc), Liabilities (GST payable, loan if any).
column6: Good chart of accounts from Day 1 prevents painful restructuring later.

column5: Build in Zoho Books or Google Sheets: monthly revenue forecast by channel, COGS by product, all operating expenses. Update actuals vs forecast monthly. Share with CA quarterly.
column6: Model must be updated monthly. A static plan is worthless. A living model is your financial compass.

column5: Week-by-week: opening cash, receipts expected (D2C orders, retail payment, corporate), payments due (ingredients, packaging, rent, CA, marketing, GST). Minimum cash floor: Rs.50,000 always.
column6: 13-week rolling cash flow is the single most important financial tool for a small business. Update every Monday.

column4: Calculate contribution margin per unit per channel
column5: For each SKU and each channel: MRP minus payment gateway fee minus shipping minus COGS = contribution per unit. D2C target: above Rs.380/unit. Retail target: above Rs.200/unit. Corporate target: above Rs.250/unit.
column6: Know your contribution before spending on CAC. Never acquire a customer at a cost that exceeds contribution.

column4: Set up unit economics tracker — update monthly
column5: One row per SKU per channel. Columns: units sold, revenue, COGS, gross profit, channel costs, contribution, CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio. Review monthly — if any cell turns red, investigate immediately.
column6: LTV:CAC below 2:1 means you are losing money acquiring customers. Catch it early.

column4: Define and document working capital cycle
column5: Map: cash paid for ingredients (Day 0) -> manufactured (Day 7-10) -> sitting as FG (Day 10-50) -> sold (Day 50) -> cash received D2C same day, retail 30-45 days. Working capital locked: 50-60 days for retail channel.
column6: Retail credit terms create working capital strain. Model this before agreeing to any retail terms.

column4: Negotiate cash-on-delivery or 15-day terms with retail chain for first 3 months
column5: First retail orders: request payment within 15 days of delivery. Do not give 45-day credit to a new retail partner before trust is established.
column6: Power dynamic: small brand needs cash more than a large retailer does. Negotiate firmly but respectfully.

column4: Set up GST input credit tracking
column5: For every purchase invoice: record GST paid (input credit). Reconcile monthly with GSTR-2B. Net GST payable = output tax minus input credit. Packaging, raw materials, equipment all have GST input.
column6: Many small brands overpay GST by not tracking input credits. This is real money recovered.

column5: All outward supplies (sales). Even if zero sales: file nil return. Late filing attracts penalty: Rs.50/day.
column6: Set calendar reminder. Never miss.

column5: Monthly summary return + tax payment. Pay GST before filing. Net payment = output minus input credit.
column6: Late filing penalty: Rs.50/day. Set payment reminder on 18th every month to ensure funds are ready.

column4: Annual GST reconciliation and audit prep
column5: By March 31: reconcile all GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B vs books. Prepare for GSTR-9 annual return. Resolve any mismatches with CA.
column6: Annual reconciliation prevents audit notices.

column4: Conduct formal price point analysis before label print
column5: Method: (1) Competitor price-per-ml table. (2) Target customer survey: show product, ask expected price (20 people). (3) Channel margin model at each price point (Rs.499 / Rs.599 / Rs.699 / Rs.799). (4) Break-even units at each price. Decision: lock MRP before any label print.
column6: Price is the hardest thing to change post-launch. One analysis session saves months of regret.

column5: If COGS has increased by more than 10%: review MRP. Compare to competitor pricing drift. Communicate price increase to retail partners 30 days in advance.
column6: Build pricing review into the calendar. Do not absorb all cost increases silently.

column4: Evaluate MSME Mudra loan for working capital
column5: Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana: Kishore category (Rs.50,000 to Rs.5 lakh) or Tarun (Rs.5 lakh to Rs.10 lakh). Available through nationalized banks. Apply with: MSME certificate, LLP documents, 6-month bank statement, business plan summary.
column6: Cost of Mudra loan: 8-12% interest. Much cheaper than credit card or personal loan. Apply only when you have 6 months of bank statements showing business activity.

column5: Page 1: Revenue, COGS, gross margin, key expense lines, net P&L vs budget. Page 2: Units by channel, customer count, NPS, top 3 wins, top 3 issues, next month priorities.
column6: This 2-page report is your board meeting with yourself. Forces clarity.

column4: Map all functions the founder is currently doing
column5: List every function: manufacturing, packing, labelling, ordering ingredients, updating website, posting on Instagram, responding to WhatsApp, doing accounts, filing GST, managing vendors, visiting retail stores. This is the burnout map.
column6: If the founder is doing more than 6 of these by Month 3, the business will stall. Identify the first function to delegate.

column4: Define first hire profile: packing and operations helper
column5: Role: 2-3 days/week. Tasks: label application, carton assembly, ingredient weighing, cleaning, basic inventory count. Must have: attention to detail, comfortable with standing work, trustworthy with product. Pay: Rs.400-500/day.
column6: Train to SOPs before first unsupervised session. Never assume a new person knows what you want.

column4: Post operations helper job — WhatsApp community / local network
column5: Post in: local WhatsApp groups, women's employment networks, Oodles staff referrals, neighbourhood networks. Interview: ask candidate to read and explain one SOP. This tests literacy and attention to detail.
column6: Sourcing from known networks reduces risk. Reference check with previous employer is always worth 10 minutes.

column5: Day 1: tour of space, introduction to all SOPs, demonstrate each packing step, supervised practice. Week 1: supervised only. Week 2: independent with spot checks. Week 3: fully independent for trained tasks.
column6: Never throw a new hire into unsupervised work on Day 1. Quality failures in the first week are the employer's fault, not the employee's.

column4: Define second hire profile: marketing executive (Month 9-12)
column5: Role: full-time. Tasks: Instagram content creation and scheduling, community management, influencer outreach coordination, monthly email newsletter, basic paid campaign management. Must have: active Instagram presence, understanding of D2C brands, interest in wellness/beauty.
column6: Pay: Rs.18,000-28,000/month. Hire on 3-month probation. First 30 days: shadow founder on all marketing tasks.

column4: Write job description for marketing executive
column5: JD components: brand description (2 paragraphs), role overview, key responsibilities (5-7 bullets), skills required, nice-to-have, what Svaas offers (flexible work, growth opportunity, brand mission). Post: LinkedIn, Instagram Stories, Internshala.
column6: A well-written JD attracts the right person and repels the wrong one. Spend 1 hour on it.

column4: Define third hire profile: operations manager (Month 18-24)
column5: Role: full-time. Owns: batch scheduling, vendor management, QC oversight, shipping, customer complaint resolution. Must have: operations or supply chain experience, manufacturing background preferred. Pay: Rs.25,000-40,000/month.
column6: This hire is what allows the founder to stop managing daily operations and focus on brand and growth.

column5: 5 principles that govern how Svaas treats its team: (1) Pay on time, always. (2) Train before you expect. (3) Explain the why, not just the what. (4) Credit work publicly. (5) Mistakes are learning when reported honestly. Post on wall in manufacturing room.
column6: Small team culture is set by behaviour, not documents. But writing it down makes it intentional.

column5: Register as employer with TN Commercial Tax Department before hiring first employee. Deduct professional tax from salary. Rs.200/month for salaries above Rs.21,000/month.
column6: Mandatory before payroll for any employee.

column4: Maintain attendance and payment records for all workers
column5: Simple register: name, date, hours worked, amount paid, signature. Required for any labour inspection.
column6: Even for part-time daily workers: keep a payment register.

column4: Model retail channel economics at different price points
column5: Build a table: at Rs.499 / Rs.599 / Rs.699 MRP — what is retailer buying price (35% margin), Svaas net revenue, minus GST 18%, minus COGS Rs.130, minus packaging Rs.35 = contribution. At which MRP is retail channel still viable?
column6: Retail margin model determines minimum viable MRP. If contribution goes negative at Rs.499 retail: Rs.499 is too low.

column5: At Rs.599 single / Rs.999 duo / Rs.1,499 trio gift set: calculate COGS of set + gift packaging (Rs.65-80) + personalisation card. Calculate Svaas net revenue at each price. Corporate discount: typically 15-20% off MRP for orders of 25+ units.
column6: Corporate gifting should achieve 55-65% gross margin even after discount. Model confirms pricing.

column5: Documents: (1) Company profile 1-page. (2) GSTIN certificate. (3) Cosmetic licence copy. (4) Product catalogue with HSN codes and MRP. (5) Trade price list (MRP minus 35%). (6) Test reports summary. (7) Label compliance declaration. (8) Return and replenishment policy.
column6: Retail buyers get pitched by 20 brands a week. A complete, professional documentation pack signals that you are serious.

column4: Prepare retail display solution for all stores
column5: Counter-top display unit: acrylic or foam, holds 3 SKUs, header card with Svaas branding and key claim. One tester unit per SKU (sealed with 'TESTER' label). Order 15 units for 15-store chain. Cost: Rs.800-1,500 per unit.
column6: Products without display units are invisible on shelf. Display is not optional for a new brand.

column4: Train retail store staff on Svaas product
column5: Create 1-page retail staff cheat sheet: what Svaaskriti is, who it is for, top 3 customer questions with answers, how to demonstrate (1 pump on hand). Visit each store in first month. Personal relationship with store manager drives sell-through.
column6: The person behind the counter is your most valuable salesperson. Invest 30 minutes per store.

column4: Set up monthly retail replenishment system
column5: On 1st of each month: check stock levels at all active stores (call or visit). Reorder threshold: when shelf drops to 3 units per SKU. Send fresh stock within 3-5 working days. Invoice on delivery.
column6: Retail brands get delisted when they are frequently out of stock. Be the most reliable supplier in their section.

column4: Track sell-through rate per store monthly
column5: Measure: units placed vs units sold in 30 days at each store. Target sell-through: above 60% per month. If below 40% for 2 consecutive months: review placement, pricing, or staff knowledge.
column6: Sell-through is the true measure of retail success. Not units delivered — units bought by customers.

column5: Every 3 months with retail buyer: review sell-through data, discuss any issues, propose next season plan, discuss whether additional SKUs make sense.
column6: Relationship management is what keeps you on shelf when a competitor pitches.

column4: Evaluate second retail chain at Day 270
column5: After first chain is running smoothly: identify 2nd potential retail partner. Criteria: different geography or different customer profile from first chain. Do not spread too thin too early.
column6: Second retail partnership only when first is operationally stable.

column4: Build corporate prospect database in Google Sheet
column5: Fields: company name, industry, size, HR/Admin contact name, LinkedIn URL, email, phone, status, last contact date, opportunity (Diwali/Pongal/Women's Day/New Year), estimated order size.
column6: Start with 25 prospects. Grow to 100 by Day 180. Quality of contact matters more than quantity.

column4: Send personalised outreach to 25 corporate prospects
column5: Channel: LinkedIn DM + WhatsApp if contact known personally. Message: 2 sentences about Svaas, 1 sentence about gifting, link to gifting catalogue PDF, offer of Rs.99 sample set. Not a mass blast — each message personalised.
column6: Personalisation converts 3-5x better than mass email for B2B gifting. Takes more time but worth it.

column5: Target: first order of minimum 25 units by Day 120. Offer: free customised note card, delivery within 7 days, invoice with GST.
column6: First corporate order is the proof case. Document it as a case study.

column4: Build corporate gifting case study after first order
column5: After delivery: call the HR contact. Ask: how was the response from employees? Can you share a quote? Take a photo of the gift hamper. This becomes a testimonial for the next 25 prospects.
column6: B2B buyers trust other B2B buyers. One case study is worth 10 sales pitches.

column5: Diwali (Oct): book by Sept 15. Pongal (Jan): book by Dec 20. Women's Day (Mar 8): book by Feb 20. New Year (Jan 1): book by Dec 15. Raksha Bandhan (Aug): evaluate. Create reminder-based outreach 6 weeks before each festival.
column6: Corporate gifting is calendar-driven. Miss the booking window and the budget is spent elsewhere.

column4: Offer custom branding option from Month 6
column5: Option: company name printed on note card, or Svaas gift box with subtle company logo sticker. Minimum order: 50 units for customisation. Premium: Rs.25-50 per unit extra.
column6: Custom branding converts larger orders. Many corporates have budget for this.

column5: Content: (1) What Svaaskriti is (2 sentences). (2) Why salons love it: adds a premium scalp treatment to their menu, Rs.300-600 per client, product cost only Rs.35-45 per treatment. (3) Testimonials from pilot salons. (4) How to order and pricing.
column6: Salon owners think in profit per chair per hour. Show them the math clearly.

column5: Same formula. 200ml amber PET with professional pump. Label: 'Svaas Svaaskriti — Professional Salon Use.' Wholesale price: Rs.450-500. Salon MRP equivalent: Rs.899-999.
column6: Salon buyers want professional-looking product separate from retail. A 200ml bottle signals professional use.

column4: Visit each pilot salon after 2-week trial
column5: Personal visit. Questions: Did you use it? On which clients? What was their feedback? Would you add it to your treatment menu? What would make it more useful for you?
column6: This conversation is worth more than any sales pitch. Listen, do not sell.

column4: Formalise commercial terms with 3+ willing salons
column5: After positive trial feedback: propose commercial arrangement. Wholesale price: Rs.450/200ml. Payment: 15 days after delivery. Replenishment: monthly or on-demand. No sale-or-return for salon channel (product is used, not resold).
column6: Start with 3 salons. Nail the model before expanding to 10.

column5: One laminated card per salon: what Svaaskriti is, application technique for scalp massage, leave-on time (minimum 30 mins for treatment), wash-out, what to tell clients about the 61 ingredients. Give to every stylist.
column6: Salon staff talk to 8-12 clients per day. A well-briefed stylist is a Rs.0 brand ambassador.

column4: Offer salon testers for client waiting area
column5: Small 10ml sample bottles labelled 'Try Svaaskriti.' Placed in waiting area or at shampoo station. Cost: Rs.15-20 per 10ml sample. Drives client curiosity and retail conversion.
column6: Salon retail conversion is 3x higher when clients have touched and smelled the product before deciding.

column4: Research target boutique hotel and resort partners
column5: Identify: boutique hotels in Chennai, Pondicherry, Coorg, Kovalam, Mahabalipuram that have Ayurvedic or wellness positioning. Look for: spa menus that include hair treatments, in-room amenity programmes, gift shop retail.
column6: 20 target hotels is a good start list. Quality: hotels where guests are willing to pay for wellness.

column5: 2-page document: (1) Brand story and product overview. (2) Three partnership models: in-room amenity (50-100ml in bathroom), spa treatment product, gift shop retail. (3) Pricing per model. (4) Minimum quantities.
column6: Hotels evaluate amenity brands on: scent, texture, packaging quality, brand story, exclusivity. Lead with these.

column5: Start with 1 hotel, 30 rooms, 60-unit pilot (2 units per room). Negotiate: hotel mentions Svaas in room collateral. Svaas provides: branded card explaining product. Review after 30 days: guest feedback, refill order.
column6: Hotel partnership validates premium positioning. 1 successful hotel pilot = credibility for next 10.

column4: Identify Chennai pop-up and market calendar for Year 1
column5: Research: Chennai Farmers Market, Namma Chennai Market, WOW Chennai, Organic Markets, Women Entrepreneur fairs. Get dates, stall costs, audience profile, application process. Map to Svaas launch calendar.
column6: Pop-up markets are ideal for pre-launch brand awareness and post-launch early customers. First event: Day 90-120 at earliest.

column5: Components: (1) Branded table cover or banner (Rs.1,500-3,000). (2) Product display risers. (3) Tester set with mini-bottles. (4) QR code standee linking to Shopify. (5) WhatsApp signup sheet or QR. (6) Business cards. (7) Gift packaging display.
column6: First impression at a stall is visual. Invest in a clean, branded setup.

column5: 3-sentence opener: 'This is Svaaskriti — 61 natural ingredients, no chemicals, handmade in Chennai. It is food for your hair — complete nourishment, not a treatment. Would you like to smell it?' Then listen.
column6: Practise the script. A founder who sounds rehearsed sounds confident, not salesy.

column4: Set up lead collection system at exhibitions
column5: Use: Google Form QR code at stall. Collect: name, phone, hair type, email. Offer: Rs.50 off first online order for signing up. Follow up within 48 hours with WhatsApp message.
column6: Exhibition without lead collection = one-time interaction. Lead collection turns a conversation into a customer.

column5: Within 24 hours: WhatsApp message to all leads: 'Great to meet you at [event]. Here is your Rs.50 discount code: [code].' Within 48 hours: Instagram follow request. Within 7 days: if no purchase, send 1 follow-up message.
column6: Most exhibition leads go cold after 48 hours. Speed of follow-up determines conversion.

column5: For each event: cost (stall + materials + product) vs revenue on day + revenue from leads within 30 days. If ROI is below 2x: evaluate whether to continue with that event type.
column6: Not all exhibitions are equal. Track the data and invest only in events that convert.

column5: Score each potential influencer: (1) Follower count (weight: 10%). (2) Engagement rate — likes+comments/followers (weight: 40%). (3) Content quality and aesthetic match (weight: 30%). (4) Audience location — Chennai/metro focus (weight: 20%). Minimum score 70/100 to proceed.
column6: Follower count is the least important metric. Engagement rate is the most important.

column4: Send personalised gifting DM — not a template
column5: Message structure: (1) Specific compliment about their content (not generic). (2) Why Svaas product feels right for their audience (specific). (3) Offer to send product with zero expectation — just want them to experience it. (4) If they love it, an honest post would mean the world.
column6: Template DMs get ignored. Personalised DMs get responses. Takes 5 minutes extra per influencer.

column5: Contents: product in full packaging, handwritten note from founder (personalised), 1-page 'story card' with Svaas brand story, ingredient card (top 5 ingredients), no required hashtags, no script — just ask for honest impressions.
column6: The handwritten note from the founder is the most powerful element. It signals this is a real person, not a marketing team.

column4: Track influencer content and attribute orders
column5: For each influencer post: note date, reach, engagement, discount code used (create unique code per influencer), orders attributed. Calculate: cost per order from each influencer relationship.
column6: Unique discount codes per influencer track attribution perfectly. Use these to identify your top 3 performing influencer relationships.

column4: Re-engage top 3 performing nano-influencers at Month 6
column5: Send a second product (new SKU or seasonal gift set) to the 3 influencers who drove most orders. No paid arrangement required yet. Continue gifting relationship. Personal thank you note.
column6: Repeat content from the same influencer is more powerful than first-time content — it signals genuine loyalty.

column4: Evaluate first paid influencer collaboration at Day 270
column5: By Day 270: if brand is established and revenue is above Rs.1 lakh/month, evaluate 1 paid collaboration with a mid-tier influencer (50K-200K followers, wellness niche). Brief: authentic review, Food For Hair narrative, before/after welcome but not required.
column6: Paid influencer rate: Rs.5,000-25,000 per post for this tier. Calculate expected ROI before committing.

column5: Monthly themes: M1=Foundations (brand story), M2=Ingredients (61+ series begins), M3=Ritual (how to use), M4=Results (first testimonials), M5=Science (Ayurveda meets modern), M6=Community (UGC), M7=Gifting (corporate season), M8=Seasonal (post-monsoon hair care), M9=Deep dive (hero ingredients), M10=Customer love, M11=Festival gifting, M12=Year in review.
column6: 12 months of themes gives the content calendar direction without being rigid. Each month has a north star.

column5: For each of the 57 documented ingredients: post a carousel with: English name + Tamil name + Sanskrit name, where it comes from, what it does for hair, why it is in Svaaskriti, how traditional use compares to modern evidence. 57 posts = 14 months of weekly content.
column6: This series is uniquely Svaas. No competitor has 57 individually documented ingredients. It is both SEO content and brand differentiation.

column4: Create Hair Ritual Education series (8 posts)
column5: 8-post carousel series: (1) Why oil your hair at all? (2) The difference between hair treatment and hair nourishment. (3) How to apply oil correctly (scalp focus). (4) How long to leave oil on. (5) How to wash out properly. (6) Frequency: daily vs weekly. (7) What to expect in Week 1, Month 1, Month 3. (8) Signs your hair is getting healthier.
column6: This series educates customers on HOW to use Svaaskriti and sets correct expectations. Reduces dissatisfaction from incorrect use.

column4: Create 'What 61 ingredients actually means' hero post
column5: One definitive carousel post that explains: why 61 ingredients (not marketing gimmick), the three groups (fresh botanicals, dry herbs, essential oils), the traditional process, the modern science validation. This is the post that converts sceptics.
column6: Pin this post to the Instagram profile. It is the brand's most important piece of content.

column5: Connect to website domain. Verify. Submit sitemap. Monitor: which search queries bring visitors, which pages rank, crawl errors.
column6: Free tool. Essential for understanding how people find Svaas through Google.

column5: Categories: Ayurvedic hair oil (high volume), natural hair oil Chennai (local), hair nourishment oil (aligned to positioning), 61 ingredient hair oil (branded), handmade hair oil India. Use Google Keyword Planner (free) or Ubersuggest.
column6: Start SEO early. Results take 3-6 months. Plant seeds now for Year 1 harvest.

column4: Publish first SEO blog article: 'Why Ayurvedic Hair Oiling Works'
column5: 1,500 words. Target keyword: 'Ayurvedic hair oil benefits.' Structure: history of hair oiling in India, science behind oil and scalp health, 5 key ingredients and their evidence, how to build a hair oiling ritual, why Svaaskriti is different. Internal link to product page.
column6: Blog articles compound. This article written today may bring 100 visitors/month in Year 2.

column4: Publish SEO blog: 'The 61 Ingredients in Svaaskriti — Complete Guide'
column5: Deep-dive article linking to the ingredient library. 2,000+ words. Target keyword: 'Svaaskriti ingredients.' Every ingredient mentioned by name = long-tail keyword capture.
column6: This article will rank for ingredient name searches. Someone searching 'Jatamasi for hair' should find Svaas.

column5: Challenge: use Svaaskriti 3x per week for 8 weeks. Post weekly photo with hashtag. Founder posts weekly tips and encouragement. Winner: best transformation story wins 6-month supply. First challenge: Day 120-270.
column6: Challenge creates: accountability, community, UGC content, 8-week engagement arc, and proof of results. All in one programme.

column4: Build WhatsApp community for Svaas customers
column5: Separate from broadcast list. Invite customers who have purchased twice or more. Community guidelines: share your hair journey, ask questions, no spam. Founder active in community weekly.
column6: A community of 50 engaged customers is more valuable than 5,000 passive followers. Community = retention.

column4: Set up Shopify inventory management properly
column5: Enable inventory tracking per SKU. Set low stock alerts at 6-week threshold. Connect to Shiprocket for auto-fulfilment. Test: does inventory decrement correctly when order is placed?
column6: Many small brands have Shopify but do not configure inventory correctly. Overselling happens when this is skipped.

column4: Create dispatch log in Google Sheets
column5: Columns: date, order ID, customer name, SKU, quantity, batch number used, courier AWB number, dispatch confirmed (Y/N). Update daily. This log is your traceability record.
column6: When a customer complaint comes in with batch number, this log tells you within 60 seconds who else received that batch.

column4: Create colour reference standard from first approved batch
column5: After first QC-approved batch of hair oil: retain a sealed 10ml vial. Label: 'Colour Reference Standard — Batch SVHOP-001.' Every subsequent batch compared to this. If batch colour is more than 20% darker or lighter: investigate before dispatching.
column6: Colour drift between batches signals: ingredient substitution, temperature variation, or infusion time change. Catch it at QC not after dispatch.

column5: Purchase rubber stamp set with replaceable numbers. Stamp batch number on label before application. Format: SVHOP-YYYYMMDD-001. Check stamp is legible on 10 test labels before each stamping session.
column6: The batch number on each bottle is the link between customer and your manufacturing records. It must be legible.

column4: Negotiate Shiprocket/Delhivery rates for prepaid orders
column5: As volume grows: negotiate with Shiprocket account manager for better per-shipment rates. Benchmark: 100g-300g shipment should cost Rs.55-75 within South India, Rs.70-90 pan-India.
column6: Shipping cost is a direct margin eroder. Negotiate after first 50 shipments when you have volume data.

column5: Designate specific days/times for packing. Example: Tuesday and Friday, 10am-2pm. Batch packing is more efficient than packing 1-2 orders ad hoc every day. Pre-stage all materials the night before packing sessions.
column6: Batch packing 20 orders in 2 hours is more efficient than packing 2 orders 10 times a week. Reduces setup/teardown time.

column5: Columns: date, order ID, customer, reason for return, batch number, condition of returned product, resolution (refund/replace/reject), amount refunded, date resolved.
column6: Returns data tells you: most common failure mode, any quality pattern (all from one batch?), whether refund policy is being abused.

column4: Write WhatsApp customer service response scripts
column5: Create saved replies for: (1) Order status query. (2) Ingredient question. (3) 'Will it work for my hair type?' (4) Return request. (5) Complaint about product. (6) Bulk/corporate order inquiry. Each script: personal tone, under 100 words, ends with a question or action.
column6: Saved replies reduce response time from 30 minutes to 30 seconds while maintaining quality.

column4: Set up post-delivery review request flow
column5: Klaviyo: Day 10 after delivery — automated email: 'How is your hair feeling after your first 10 days with Svaaskriti?' Single-question form + link to leave a Shopify review + link to leave a Google review.
column6: Day 10 is peak engagement. Before then: too early. After Day 20: interest drops. Hit the window precisely.
