column4: Conduct 10 in-depth customer interviews before launch
column5: Select: 5 beta testers who gave positive feedback + 5 who gave neutral/mixed feedback. 30-minute conversation (in person or video). Questions: tell me about your hair journey, what have you tried before, what disappointed you, what would make you a loyal customer of any brand, what made you try Svaaskriti?
column6: Qualitative interviews reveal insights that forms cannot. The exact words customers use become your best marketing copy.

column5: 10 open-ended questions. Avoid: leading questions. Include: tell me about a time, what frustrated you most, if you could change one thing. Record with permission. Transcribe key quotes.
column6: The interview guide makes every conversation yield comparable insights.

column4: Build customer persona profiles from interview data
column5: After 10 interviews: identify patterns. Which persona resonates most with actual buyers? Update the 4 personas in the Customer & Market sheet based on real data, not assumptions.
column6: Personas built from real interviews are 10x more useful than assumed personas.

column4: Deploy 30-day customer survey for first 50 customers
column5: At Day 30 of ownership: 5-question survey via Klaviyo. Questions: (1) How often are you using it? (2) What is your hair feeling like? (3) One word to describe Svaaskriti? (4) Would you buy again? (5) One thing we could improve.
column6: Day 30 data from first 50 customers is your most important early dataset. Use it to refine messaging and identify product improvements.

column4: Set up repeat customer analysis monthly
column5: In Shopify: tag customers who purchase 2+ times. Monthly: count repeat customers, calculate repeat rate %, identify which SKU combination drives repeat, note average time between first and second order.
column6: Repeat purchase pattern tells you: is the product working? Are customers running out and reordering? Or are they forgetting about it?

column4: Quarterly NPS survey to full customer base
column5: Send via Klaviyo: 'How likely are you to recommend Svaas to a friend?' 1-10 scale + one open text question. Calculate NPS: (% Promoters minus % Detractors). Target above 50 from Day 180.
column6: NPS above 50 is excellent for a small brand. Below 30: investigate root cause urgently.

column5: Colours: exact hex codes for plum/wine + cream/off-white. Fonts: primary (headings) + secondary (body). Logo usage rules: minimum size, clear space, forbidden uses. Photography style: mood board with 10 reference images. Tone of voice: 5 adjectives that describe how Svaas writes.
column6: Anyone creating anything for Svaas — Oodles, a freelancer, a packaging vendor — gets this guide. Consistency across every touchpoint is a brand.

column4: Create master brand asset folder structure
column5: Google Drive: (1) Logos (all formats: PNG, SVG, black, white, colour). (2) Photography (full-res, web-optimised). (3) Video (brand film, how-to). (4) Templates (Instagram posts, Stories, email). (5) Documents (Brand Constitution, Style Guide, Press Kit). Share access with: Founder, QP (for label work), CA (for official docs).
column6: If the laptop dies tomorrow, are all brand assets recoverable? This folder is your insurance.

column5: 3 versions of founder story: (1) 30-second elevator pitch. (2) 2-minute media story. (3) 5-minute long-form story. All anchored to: the Chennai beach moment, the 61 ingredients, the all-women team, the Food For Hair philosophy.
column6: Journalists ask the founder story question. Being prepared with 3 formats means you never stumble in an interview.

column4: Build media contact list — beauty and wellness journalists
column5: Target: 15 journalists covering beauty, wellness, D2C brands, women entrepreneurs. Platforms: Vogue India, Femina, GoodHomes, Forbes India Women, Inc42, YourStory, The Hindu MetroPlus, Behindwoods.
column6: PR is a long game. Start building media relationships 60 days before you need them.

column5: First 100 customers are designated Svaas Founding Members. They receive: (1) Personal note from founder. (2) Founding Member discount (10% off for life). (3) Early access to new products. (4) Their name on the Svaas website 'Our Story' page (with consent). This costs almost nothing but creates extraordinary loyalty.
column6: The first 100 customers of any brand are its most valuable. Treat them accordingly.

column5: Add renewal date to Compliance Master tracker. Set calendar reminder 90 days before expiry. Start renewal process 60 days before expiry.
column6: Expired licence = illegal manufacturing. This reminder is worth setting up once on Day 1.

column4: Conduct microbial testing on every 3rd batch
column5: After initial batch testing passes: retest every 3rd batch. If any batch fails: test every batch until 5 consecutive passes, then revert to every 3rd batch.
column6: Ongoing testing is the difference between knowing and assuming your product is safe.

column5: Once per year: send a sample from the latest batch for full heavy metals panel. Compare against original result.
column6: Ingredient quality can change over time. Annual retest ensures continued compliance.

column5: File must contain: MFR, SOP set, test reports (all), stability data, label specimens, batch records. Inspector can ask for this at any time. Keep physical file + digital backup.
column6: This is your compliance insurance policy. Keep it current.

column5: Once per year: review all website copy, all Instagram captions, all packaging text, all sales materials. Check: any claim that has crept into drug territory? Any before/after that makes an implicit treatment promise? Clean up.
column6: Claim drift happens slowly. Annual audit catches it before it becomes a regulatory issue.

column5: On last Friday of every month: review innovation pipeline sheet. Any customer feedback suggesting a new product? Any market gap identified this month? Any ingredient spotted that is interesting? Log all in the pipeline. Do not act on all of them.
column6: The innovation pipeline should grow continuously. Acting on it: selectively, with evidence.

column5: Every 3 months: buy and evaluate 2-3 competitor new launches or interesting products. Evaluate: formulation, packaging, claims, pricing, unboxing experience. Note: what can Svaas learn?
column6: Not to copy — to stay informed. What is the market doing? Where is Svaas ahead? Where behind?

column4: Formal product gate review at each phase milestone
column5: Before any new SKU is added to the active launch plan: it must pass the 4-question gate: (1) Does it improve AOV? (2) Does it improve gifting? (3) Does it improve retention? (4) Does it have a ritual story? Minimum 2 of 4 required to proceed. Document the gate review outcome.
column6: This gate prevents product proliferation. Every SKU must earn its place.

column5: Agenda: (1) Deliverables due this week — status check. (2) Blockers — what does Oodles need from Founder? (3) Next week plan. (4) Any quality issues with recent deliverables? Standing call, same time every week.
column6: 30-minute weekly sync prevents 3-hour monthly catch-up sessions and delivery surprises.

column4: Monthly Oodles deliverable review and billing reconciliation
column5: First Monday of month: review completed deliverables vs plan. Confirm billing for month. Identify any scope creep. Update next month plan.
column6: Formal monthly review prevents billing disputes and scope drift.

column5: Log monthly: studio time used for Svaas photography, meeting room time, printing costs, software licences shared. Calculate fair value of infrastructure contribution. Even if not billed, this should be tracked for investor/CA transparency.
column6: Transparent cost tracking of shared resources protects both parties and gives accurate Svaas unit economics.

column4: Content quality review — compliance check before posting
column5: Before any Instagram post, email, or website copy goes live: 60-second compliance check. Ask: does this make a drug claim? Does this use before/after language that implies treatment? Does this align with the Brand Constitution? If any answer is yes: flag to Founder before posting.
column6: One non-compliant post can trigger a Drug Controller notice. 60 seconds of review prevents this.

column4: GMP certification — initiate process at Day 365
column5: ISO 22716 Good Manufacturing Practice certification. Required for: export to EU/UAE, major retail chain listing (some require it), Nykaa premium seller programme. Timeline: 3-6 months. Engage a GMP consultant.
column6: GMP is not just a certificate — it upgrades every process in the business. Start Year 2 with this.

column5: Apply at DGFT portal: fee Rs.500, 2-3 working days. Required before any export. No reason not to do this at Day 365 — it is quick, cheap, and enables future export options.
column6: File this even if export is 18 months away. It takes 2 days and costs Rs.500.

column5: UAE has the largest Indian diaspora in the world. Premium Ayurvedic hair oil has strong NRI appeal. Requirements: UAE product registration (CPNP-equivalent), Arabic label, UAE importer/distributor. Engage export consultant.
column6: Start research at Day 450. First export consignment realistic at Day 540-600.

column5: Apply after: 100+ website reviews, Rs.1 lakh/month revenue, GMP certification. Prepare: brand story doc, licence copy, test reports, product photography (white background, Nykaa format), product descriptions in Nykaa format.
column6: Nykaa brings discovery to a national audience. Apply when you are ready — not before.

column5: Annual review: revenue vs plan, top 10 learnings, what worked beyond expectation, what did not work, product portfolio review, customer insights summary, Year 2 priorities (3 max). Write a 1-page Year 1 summary document.
column6: The Year 1 retrospective is the single most valuable planning session of the business. Block a full day for it.

column4: Lock brand name this week — numerology shortlist to MCA check
column5: Step 1: produce shortlist of 3 names from numerology or naming process. Step 2: for each name — check MCA (mca.gov.in company search), IP India Class 3 search, domain .in availability. Step 3: pick the name that clears all three. Step 4: log decision in Decision Register with rationale.
column6: This is the Day 1 task. Nothing can proceed — LLP, domain, trademark, label — until the name is locked.

column4: Commit to soft launch date in calendar
column5: Pick a specific target date for soft launch (Day 90-120 range). Write it down. Tell 5 people. This creates accountability and works backward to create urgency on every preceding task.
column6: A target without a date is a wish. A target with a date is a commitment.

column4: Define personal success metrics for Year 1
column5: Write down: what does success look like at the end of Year 1? Specific numbers: revenue, customers, NPS, channels, team. These become the KPI targets. Review quarterly. Revise if needed.
column6: Founder clarity on success metrics prevents drift and prevents measuring the wrong things.

column4: Protect minimum 1 day per week from operations
column5: Designate one day per week (Sunday, or any day) where Founder does no packing, no customer service, no WhatsApp. For strategic thinking, rest, and creative work only. Write this into the weekly schedule from Month 1.
column6: Founder burnout is the single largest business risk for Svaas. Structure rest before it becomes a crisis.

column4: Set up monthly 1-hour founder review ritual
column5: First Friday of each month, 10am-11am. Same time every month. Agenda is in the Founder Register sheet. Close the laptop, no phone, just the numbers and a notepad. Write 3 priorities for next month at the end of every session.
column6: This one hour per month is worth more than 40 hours of reactive work. It is the only time to think about the business rather than in it.

column4: Annual 1-day strategic offsite (solo or with advisor)
column5: Once per year: take a full day away from the office. Review: where did the business go vs plan? What are the 3 biggest strategic choices for next year? What should Svaas stop doing? What should it double down on? Write a 1-page Year Ahead document.
column6: Annual thinking time is what separates brands that drift from brands that build intentionally.

column5: One document containing: all vendor contacts and backup contacts, formula vault location, all login credentials (stored in a password manager, shared with one trusted person), bank account details, CA contact, regulatory consultant contact, QP contact. Store: Google Drive + physical copy in locked location.
column6: If the founder is unreachable for 2 weeks, could someone keep the business running? This document makes that possible.

column5: Every month: add new opportunities heard during the month to the backlog in the Founder Register sheet. Remove opportunities that are no longer relevant. Pick 1 opportunity to evaluate formally each quarter.
column6: The backlog prevents good ideas from getting lost while ensuring the present is not distracted by future possibilities.

column4: Visit Koyambedu wholesale market for herb evaluation
column5: In-person visit to Chennai's Koyambedu wholesale market. Purpose: identify vendors for dried herbs (Bhringraj, Amla, Hibiscus, Curry Leaves, Methi, Shikakai). Get 3 prices per ingredient. Buy small samples. Note: vendor name, stall number, mobile.
column6: Personal relationships with market vendors get better quality and better prices than buying blind.

column4: Source Siddha medicine ingredients from specialist suppliers
column5: Visit: Nattar Pettai area, Chennai Bypass Road Siddha suppliers. Required: Magizhmpoo (Spanish Cherry Flower), Anjanakal (Surma Stone), Korai Kilangu (Nut Grass), Ponnanganni. These cannot be found at general herb markets.
column6: These rare Tamil ingredients are your brand differentiators. Secure supply before committing to them in formula.

column4: Request Certificate of Analysis from all EO vendors
column5: For every essential oil ordered: request CoA showing: botanical name, country of origin, specific gravity, refractive index, GC/MS result. Reject any lot without CoA. File all CoAs in compliance folder.
column6: CoA is the only way to verify EO authenticity. Adulterated EOs are common in the Indian market.

column4: Build direct relationship with 1 Kannauj EO supplier
column5: Travel to or video call with a Kannauj supplier (SN Perfumeries or similar). Place first order. Request: frankincense, bergamot, lavender, patchouli, ylang ylang, vetiver. Get pricing for 100g and 500g quantities.
column6: Direct Kannauj relationship = 30-40% lower cost than buying EOs through Chennai distributors.

column5: Google Sheet: for each batch, record exact quantity of each ingredient used vs MFR quantity. Monthly total consumption by ingredient. This builds 6-month consumption data to predict reorder quantities accurately.
column6: After 3 months of data: reorder quantities become precise instead of estimated.

column4: Set minimum stock levels for all Tier 1 ingredients
column5: For each Tier 1 ingredient: calculate minimum stock = (batches per month x quantity per batch x lead time in months) + safety buffer. Write on label of storage container. Never go below this level.
column6: Visual minimum stock markers prevent the most common operational crisis: discovering an ingredient is out the morning of a planned batch.

column5: On the 25th of each month: review current RM inventory, planned batches for next month, current stock vs minimum levels. Generate list of ingredients to order. Place all orders by 28th to ensure delivery before month-end.
column6: Batch procurement once a month is more efficient than ad-hoc daily ordering.

column4: Identify and pre-qualify 2 backup vendors per Tier 1 ingredient
column5: For coconut oil, sesame oil, jojoba oil, argan oil: identify and order a small sample from a backup vendor. Confirm: quality acceptable, delivery reliable. Keep contact active with small quarterly orders if needed.
column6: A backup vendor list that was never tested is not a backup. Pre-qualify by actually ordering.

column5: For every ingredient lot rejected (wrong colour, wrong odour, weight discrepancy, no CoA): log: date, vendor, ingredient, quantity, reason for rejection, action taken (returned/destroyed). Share with vendor on rejection.
column6: Rejection logs create accountability with vendors. A vendor who gets 2 rejections in a row either improves or loses the account.

column4: Conduct first full production run with BMR documentation
column5: First batch after licence and MFR are locked: run the complete hair oil process with BMR filled in real time. QP present. Every step timed and documented. This run validates the SOP and establishes the baseline.
column6: The first documented production run is the most important one. Every subsequent run is measured against it.

column5: Theoretical yield: 10L batch = 100 units of 100ml. Actual yield after losses (filtration, transfer, filling waste): measure carefully. Calculate yield percentage. Target: above 92%.
column6: Yield below 90% means either the SOP has waste inefficiencies or the fill volume is inconsistent.

column4: Time each production stage and log in BMR
column5: For first 3 batches: time each stage. Fresh ingredient cooking: how many minutes? Infusion: exactly how many hours? Cooling: how long? Filling 100 bottles: how long? Total batch time: how many hours?
column6: Time data becomes the production planning input. Without it, batch scheduling is guesswork.

column4: Create visual SOP cards for manufacturing room wall
column5: Print and laminate A4 cards for: (1) Batch numbering format. (2) EO addition temperature limit (40 degrees C — in large red text). (3) QC checkpoint checklist. (4) Emergency contacts. Post at eye level in manufacturing area.
column6: Visual cues in the manufacturing space prevent errors better than written documents that live in a folder.

column5: With current setup (20L vessel x2, 1 operator): maximum batches per week = 2 (each batch is 1 full day). Maximum units per month = 200. Document this as the Phase 1 capacity ceiling.
column6: Knowing the ceiling prevents over-promising on delivery timelines.

column5: Trigger for Upgrade 1 (add second 20L vessel): orders exceed 150/month for 2 consecutive months. Trigger for Upgrade 2 (add 50L vessel): orders exceed 400/month. Trigger for Upgrade 3 (dedicated space): monthly revenue exceeds Rs.5 lakh consistently.
column6: Pre-define upgrade triggers so the decision is not made reactively in a crisis.

column4: Manufacture first bath salt test batches (Heart and Root Chakra)
column5: Make 20-unit test batches of each variant. Test: dissolution in warm water (complete within 2 minutes), fragrance release, no oil separation, no colour staining on white towel, skin feel after 15-minute soak.
column6: Bath salt test is quick (2 hours per batch). Complete before committing to full packaging run.

column4: Photograph each stage of first production run
column5: Take photos: ingredient weights on scale, vessels in use, filtration, filling, finished product. These photos (a) document the process for training future staff, (b) become behind-the-scenes Instagram content, (c) show regulators your process.
column6: One 2-hour photo session at first production run creates 3 months of behind-the-scenes content.

column4: Define specific outcome metrics for beta testing
column5: Before distributing beta bottles, define: what counts as positive feedback? What are the 5 measurable outcomes to track? Suggested: (1) reduced hair fall count (can use comb test), (2) scalp itch/dryness rating 1-10, (3) shine assessment (testers photograph in natural light), (4) frizz 1-5 rating, (5) fragrance satisfaction 1-5.
column6: Without pre-defined metrics, beta feedback is vague. With metrics, it is evidence.

column4: Create standardised before-photo protocol for beta testers
column5: Send instructions: (1) Clean hair, no product. (2) Natural daylight, same spot every time. (3) Photo from same 3 angles: front, left side, right side. (4) Photo after oiling — note any visible scalp improvement.
column6: Standardised photos are comparable. Random photos are not. This protocol takes 10 minutes to write and saves hours of unusable before/afters.

column4: Debrief each salon after 2-week professional trial
column5: For each salon: scheduled 20-minute call. Questions: (1) Which clients did you use it on? (2) What was their immediate feedback after treatment? (3) Did any clients ask to purchase? (4) What would you change about the product or packaging for salon use? (5) Would you add a scalp treatment service using Svaaskriti to your menu?
column6: Salon professional feedback is qualitatively different from consumer feedback. More technical, more honest.

column4: Conduct informal retail user test at Day 150
column5: At first retail store that agrees to stock: observe 3 customers who pick up the product. Do they read the label? Do they smell the tester? What makes them put it back? What makes them buy? Do not intervene. Just observe.
column6: Shelf behaviour observation reveals purchase barriers that no survey or interview can.

column4: Use first exhibition as structured test
column5: At first exhibition (Day 90-120): track: (1) How many people stopped at stall? (2) How many smelled the product? (3) What was the most common question asked? (4) How many bought on the day? (5) Most common objection heard? Log in real time on phone notes.
column6: Every exhibition is also market research. Structure the observation before the event.

column4: Build objection database from all testing channels
column5: Maintain a running list of every objection heard: from beta testers, exhibitions, retail observation, WhatsApp enquiries, Instagram comments. Categorise: price, fragrance, efficacy doubt, ingredient concern, competitor loyalty. Update monthly. Use to improve content and product.
column6: The objection database is a goldmine. Every objection is a conversion opportunity if addressed correctly.

column5: Klaviyo: 45 days after first purchase, if customer has NOT purchased again — send: 'Running low on Svaaskriti? Your hair has had 45 days of nourishment. Keep the ritual going.' Single CTA button to reorder. Offer: free shipping on reorder.
column6: Hair oil usage at recommended frequency: 100ml lasts 35-50 days. 45-day trigger catches the natural reorder window.

column4: Set up birthday email with special offer
column5: Klaviyo: collect birthday month at checkout (optional field). Send birthday email in birthday month: 'Happy Birthday from Svaas — a little gift for your hair' + 15% off code valid for 7 days.
column6: Birthday emails have the highest open rate of any automated email (60-80%). Personalisation at near-zero cost.

column4: Identify and personally call top 10 customers monthly
column5: In Shopify: filter by highest LTV. Top 10 customers every month get a personal WhatsApp from Founder: 'How is your hair? Anything you want to tell us?' No sales pitch. Just relationship.
column6: Personal attention from the founder is something a Rs.1,000 crore company cannot offer. Use it while you can.

column5: Ask every customer who leaves a photo review: permission to repost on Instagram. Create a template response: 'Thank you [name] — your transformation is beautiful. May we share this on our page?' Tag them. Credit them.
column6: UGC is the most trusted form of content. It costs zero rupees and converts 4-6x better than brand-created content.

column4: Create escalation protocol for complex complaints
column5: Level 1 (general query): Operations handles via WhatsApp scripts. Level 2 (product dissatisfaction): Founder personally calls within 24 hours. Level 3 (adverse reaction/serious complaint): Founder + QP + Regulatory Consultant involved within 6 hours.
column6: Clear escalation levels prevent both under-response (ignored complaints) and over-response (founder personally calling about a delayed delivery).

column4: Track which content brings new customers
column5: UTM parameters in all marketing links. Monthly review: which Instagram post, which blog article, which influencer drove the most website sessions and most purchases? Double down on what works.
column6: Without UTM tracking, you are guessing which marketing works. With it, you know.

column4: Set up Google Alerts for: Ayurvedic hair oil, natural hair care India, wellness D2C India
column5: Free automated monitoring of: competitor news, market trends, ingredient research, media coverage. Review weekly digest. Flag anything relevant to the innovation pipeline.
column6: 10 minutes of setup, 5 minutes of weekly review. Keeps founder informed without active research time.

column5: Every time a customer asks: 'Do you have a conditioner? A hair mask? A shampoo? A body lotion?' — log it. After 10 requests for the same product: add to innovation pipeline for formal evaluation.
column6: Product development driven by customer requests has a much higher success rate than product development driven by founder intuition alone.

column4: Evaluate sustainable packaging options at Day 365
column5: At Year 1: compare cost of current packaging vs: recycled label stock, FSC-certified kraft board, biodegradable shrink wrap. If premium is less than 15%: switch. Communicate switch to customers.
column6: Sustainability upgrade becomes easier to justify as revenue scales. Plan the switch at Year 1, not Year 3.

column4: Commission brand health study at Day 270
column5: Survey 50 people (mix of customers and non-customers): (1) Have you heard of Svaas? (2) What do you associate with the brand? (3) What would make you try it? (4) What would stop you? This is your first brand awareness measurement.
column6: Brand health data at Day 270 shows whether the positioning is landing. Before spending more on marketing, know what is actually being heard.

column4: Evaluate women entrepreneur grant and funding schemes
column5: Research: SIDBI Women Entrepreneur schemes, TN government women startup grants, NSIC/KVIC schemes for women-led MSME, Mahila Udyam Nidhi scheme. Many require: MSME registration, 2 years of operation, or specific sector focus.
column6: Even if not immediately eligible: understand the landscape so you are ready when you are.

column4: Plan for Rs.1 crore ARR milestone
column5: Work backward from Rs.1 Cr ARR: Rs.8.3 lakh MRR = roughly 1,200-1,500 units per month at Rs.599-650 ASP. What team, space, equipment, and marketing spend is needed? Model it at Day 365 and build the Year 2 plan around it.
column6: Rs.1 Cr ARR is the first landmark. Planning for it before you are close means you are not scrambling when you arrive.

column5: Application process: confirm no animal testing at any stage of supply chain, sign pledge, pay annual fee. PETA PETA-approved vegan: Rs.10,000-20,000. Leaping Bunny: USD 500+.
column6: Cruelty-free certification is increasingly demanded by urban premium customers. Apply at Day 180 when supply chain is documented.

column5: Shopify has built-in subscription apps (Bold Subscriptions, ReCharge). Offer: monthly auto-delivery of hair oil at 10% discount. Target: 50 active subscribers by Day 365. Subscribers have 4x higher LTV than one-time buyers.
column6: Subscription revenue is the most predictable revenue. Even 50 subscribers at Rs.539/month = Rs.26,950 guaranteed monthly.

column5: Bundle options: (1) Starter Ritual: hair oil + 1 bath salt = Rs.849 vs Rs.898 separate (saving Rs.49). (2) Complete Ritual: hair oil + 2 bath salts = Rs.1,099 vs Rs.1,197 separate. (3) Gift Ritual: same as Complete Ritual in gift box = Rs.1,299.
column6: Bundles increase AOV by 25-40% without increasing CAC. Test from Day 150.

column5: Section 1 (Natural Scent): 'Svaaskriti carries the authentic fragrance of 61 wild-harvested herbs and cold-pressed oils. No synthetic fragrance. No masking agents. This is what real nourishment smells like.' Section 2 (Natural Ingredients): 'Every ingredient in Svaaskriti is plant-derived, responsibly sourced, and chosen for what it gives your hair — not for how it looks on a label.' Section 3 (No Synthetic Fragrance): 'We believe your hair deserves honesty. Svaaskriti contains no parabens, no sulphates, no mineral oil, no synthetic fragrance. Just 61 ingredients that have nourished hair for centuries.'
column6: These 3 sections replace the Lorem ipsum placeholder on the current label. Founder must approve final wording before label goes to print.

column4: Prepare complete INCI ingredient list in descending order
column5: From MFR: rank all 61+ ingredients by their percentage in the formula (descending order by weight). Write in INCI format (International Nomenclature Cosmetic Ingredient). This is the legal requirement for the back label.
column6: INCI names are the international standard. Example: Cocos Nucifera (Coconut) Oil, Sesamum Indicum (Sesame) Seed Oil. Your regulatory consultant can confirm INCI names for rare Indian ingredients.

column4: Survey 20 target customers on price expectation
column5: Show product (or photo of packaging) to 20 people who match Persona 1 or 2. Ask: what price would you expect to pay for this? What price would make you think it is too cheap? What price would make you hesitate? Average the responses and map to pricing scenarios.
column6: Price expectation surveys remove guesswork. 20 responses take 2 hours to collect and protect months of revenue.

column4: Apply 4-question product gate to every future SKU before launch decision
column5: Before any product enters the active development pipeline: answer: (1) Does it improve AOV? (2) Does it improve gifting opportunity? (3) Does it improve customer retention? (4) Does it have a genuine ritual story? Minimum 2 of 4 = YES to proceed. Log the gate result in the Innovation Pipeline sheet.
column6: This gate is the anti-bloat mechanism. It keeps the product portfolio purposeful.

column4: Formal product roadmap review every 6 months
column5: Review the product roadmap against: actual customer feedback (what are they asking for?), actual channel needs (does retail want more SKUs?), actual financial capacity (can we absorb new SKU COGS?), actual manufacturing capacity. Update launch phase for each product in the roadmap.
column6: Product roadmap without a review cycle becomes a wish list. A reviewed roadmap is a strategic plan.

column4: Create packaging inventory tracker with reorder alerts
column5: Google Sheet: each packaging component (bottle, cap, label, carton, insert, bath salt jar, gift box). Columns: component, unit, qty on hand, reorder point, reorder qty, supplier, lead time, last order date. Set reorder point at 6 weeks of projected consumption.
column6: Running out of labels is as catastrophic as running out of ingredients. Packaging lead times are 2-4 weeks.

column4: Audit existing 1,100 PET bottles for damage
column5: Before deploying existing PET inventory: inspect each bottle. Reject any with: scratches, deformity, poor neck finish (affects cap seal). Segregate into: usable (likely 95%+), reject.
column6: Deploying a leaky bottle in the first 50 orders destroys early brand trust.

column4: Commission 50ml travel size label and packaging
column5: Once 100ml launch is stable (Day 180+): design 50ml variant. Same label design, smaller. Different carton proportion. Add to GS1 barcode list.
column6: Travel size lowers trial barrier significantly. At Rs.349-399 it is also a gifting insert and exhibition sample upgrade.

column4: Photograph all packaging components for records
column5: After final packaging versions are confirmed: photograph every component with ruler for scale. File in Google Drive: Svaas/Packaging/Approved Specs. Use as reference when reordering to ensure consistency.
column6: When reordering in 6 months, having a photo reference prevents the vendor sending the wrong spec.

column4: Monitor trademark application status every 3 months
column5: Log in to IP India portal quarterly. Check: application status (Formalities Check Pass / Examination Report issued / Opposition period / Accepted). If Examination Report issued: respond within 30 days (attorney handles).
column6: Trademark applications require active monitoring. Many applications lapse because the applicant missed a step.

column4: Sign vendor agreements with key suppliers
column5: For Tier 1 ingredient suppliers (coconut oil, EOs): get a simple purchase agreement signed. Covers: product specification, delivery timeline, quality guarantee, payment terms, batch documentation requirement.
column6: A signed vendor agreement is leverage when quality issues arise.

column4: Create standard terms and conditions for corporate gifting orders
column5: Cover: order confirmation process, customisation options and lead time, payment (advance for new clients), delivery terms, force majeure, cancellation policy. Have CA review once. Use as template for all corporate orders.
column6: Standard T&Cs prevent disputes before they arise. One professionally drafted T&C document protects all future corporate transactions.

column4: Research and comply with Legal Metrology Act for packaged goods
column5: Any packaged commodity with a declared quantity must comply with Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules 2011. Requirements: MRP including taxes, net quantity, manufacturer name and address, month and year of manufacture.
column6: Your label already has most of these. Verify with a Legal Metrology consultant or CA that the label is fully compliant.

column4: Verify consumer protection compliance for D2C
column5: Under Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020: website must display: seller name and address, GSTIN, contact for grievance redressal, return and refund policy. Verify Shopify store has all required disclosures.
column6: Non-compliance with consumer protection rules is grounds for consumer complaints and penalties.

column4: Write compelling About Us page — first person founder voice
column5: Current content is third-person. Rewrite in first person: 'I started Svaas on a Chennai afternoon when...' Include: specific founding moment, the problem you personally experienced, the solution you spent years developing, the all-women team reality, the Chennai connection. End with the mission.
column6: First-person founder stories convert 2-3x better than corporate third-person copy. Authenticity is a competitive advantage.

column4: Create detailed FAQ page — 15 questions minimum
column5: Questions to cover: Is it safe for colour-treated hair? How much to use (exact amount for different hair lengths)? How often? Can I use it daily? Does it work for men? What is the shelf life? How should I store it? Is it safe during pregnancy (consult doctor note)? Will it make my hair greasy? Why does it smell earthy? How soon will I see results?
column6: Every FAQ answered is a conversion barrier removed. The FAQ page is the second most important page after the product page.

column5: Highlights: (1) Our Story (2) How To Use (3) 61 Ingredients (4) Behind The Scenes (5) Customer Love (6) Corporate Gifting. Design consistent covers for each highlight. Update weekly.
column6: Instagram Highlights are the first thing a new profile visitor sees. They should answer: what is this brand, does it work, can I trust it.

column5: Connect Shopify product catalogue to Instagram Shopping. Tag products in feed posts. Allow direct in-app purchase journey.
column6: Instagram Shopping reduces the steps from discovery to purchase. Set it up once, benefit continuously.

column4: Create Tamil language content (30% of posts)
column5: Chennai's primary audience connects more deeply with Tamil content. Create: Tamil captions alongside English, Tamil names for ingredients in carousel posts, occasional all-Tamil posts for festivals (Pongal, Tamil New Year).
column6: A brand born in Chennai that communicates only in English misses a massive connection opportunity with its home audience.

column4: Produce manufacturing batch plan every month on 25th
column5: On 25th of each month: review upcoming month's orders forecast, current FG stock, minimum buffer requirement. Calculate: how many batches needed? On what dates? Which SKUs? Share with QP for sign-off scheduling.
column6: Batch plan on 25th allows 5 days to source any missing ingredients before month-end and full month ahead for manufacturing.

column5: Check renewal date in Compliance Master. Prepare renewal application 60 days before expiry. Submit with: current premises photos, updated equipment list, updated product list, QP confirmation letter.
column6: Licences that lapse require a fresh application (not renewal). This is expensive and leaves a gap in legal manufacturing status.

column5: Stories: daily behind-the-scenes, ingredient of the week, customer reposts, product tips, polls and questions. Stories maintain daily presence without the pressure of perfect feed posts.
column6: Stories have higher daily reach than feed posts for small accounts. Use them for consistency and community.

column4: Weekly cash flow update every Monday
column5: Every Monday morning: update the 13-week cash flow with: actual receipts from previous week, actual payments made, updated forecast for next 13 weeks. Flag to Founder if projected cash drops below Rs.50,000 floor at any week.
column6: Weekly cash flow update is the single most important financial habit for a small business. It prevents surprises.

column4: Respond to every Instagram comment and DM within 4 hours
column5: Every comment: a genuine response (not just an emoji). Every DM: a real answer within 4 hours during business hours. Set Instagram notification alerts on phone.
column6: Response rate and response speed are now ranked signals for Instagram algorithmic reach. Beyond the algorithm: every response builds a relationship.

column5: On the last day of each month: physical count of all FG units by SKU and batch. Compare to Shopify inventory count. Reconcile any discrepancy. Update Shopify inventory to match physical count.
column6: Shopify inventory drifts over time due to manual errors and returns. Monthly physical count keeps it accurate.

column5: On 1st of each month: record current price per kg for top 20 ingredients from vendor invoices. Compare to previous month. Flag any increase above 10% to Finance for COGS review.
column6: Ingredient cost creep is the most common reason small brands discover their margins have quietly eroded.
